Regulation Status: Afghanistan has no specific AI legislation or comprehensive national AI policy as of March 2026. The country continues to lack a formal regulatory framework for artificial intelligence development and deployment.
Policy Lever: No identifiable policy mechanisms, guidelines, or regulatory levers for AI governance have been established. Governance remains absent in the AI domain.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structure, designated regulatory body, or institutional framework exists for overseeing artificial intelligence in Afghanistan. Governance responsibilities remain undefined.
Actor Involvement: Limited documented involvement from government, private sector, or civil society in AI policy development. International engagement on AI issues remains minimal.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, oversight bodies, or compliance monitoring systems for AI regulation exist.

Regulation Status: Albania has no national AI regulation as of March 2026, instead relying on the EU's risk-based AI Act framework through its EU integration pathway. The country is adopting international standards including the Council of Europe's AI Convention framework.
Policy Lever: Albania's policy approach centers on human rights, democracy, and rule of law protections in AI systems, informed by EU guidelines and CoE frameworks. The country is developing institutional capacity to address future AI governance challenges.
Governance Type: Governance remains primarily organizational, with model governance emerging through EU alignment rather than domestic AI-specific institutions. Albania lacks dedicated national AI regulatory bodies with autonomous enforcement authority.
Actor Involvement: Multiple actors including the EU Commission, Council of Europe, and Albanian government institutions coordinate AI policy at regional and intergovernmental levels. Albania's EU candidacy status significantly shapes its regulatory trajectory through external institutional requirements.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal and primarily indirect through EU compliance requirements, with limited domestic AI-specific enforcement infrastructure in place.
Specific Laws: Council of Europe AI Convention (signed/ratification status pending)

Regulation Status: Algeria follows the African Union's human-rights-centered AI governance framework through regional documentation including the 2021 AI Blueprint and subsequent White Papers, but has not enacted specific national AI legislation as of March 2026. The country's approach remains aligned with continental AU initiatives rather than autonomous domestic regulation.
Policy Lever: Algeria engages with data and computing export controls as a policy mechanism through regional AU frameworks, though implementation at the national level remains limited. Policy levers are primarily aspirational and aligned with continental strategy rather than actively deployed domestic instruments.
Governance Type: Algeria's AI governance follows a case-by-case and organizational approach emphasizing private sector engagement and human-centered principles inherited from AU frameworks. Governance structures remain nascent without dedicated national AI regulatory bodies or comprehensive institutional frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Algeria participates in regional governance through the African Union (particularly the July 2024 Continental AI Strategy session) and international collaboration with G7 and UNDP, but domestic institutional actors have limited documented involvement. Governance scope is primarily regional and international rather than nationally-driven.
Enforcement Level: No active enforcement mechanisms or dedicated oversight bodies for AI regulation exist at the national level in Algeria as of March 2026.

Regulation Status: Andorra has no national AI regulation as of March 2026, instead relying on EU frameworks through its alignment with European standards. The country follows the EU's risk-based approach to AI governance and is implementing provisions from the Council of Europe's AI Framework Convention adopted in March 2024.
Policy Lever: Andorra employs a data-protection and human rights-based policy approach centered on mitigating AI risks through institutional oversight. The policy focuses on democratic values, rule of law protections, and alignment with Council of Europe standards rather than sector-specific regulation.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational and model-based, delegated to EU-level and Council of Europe frameworks rather than domestic institutional structures. Andorra lacks dedicated national AI governance bodies, instead relying on existing regulatory frameworks adapted from European standards.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the EU regulatory framework, the Council of Europe (through the AI Convention), and Andorran alignment mechanisms at the intergovernmental level. Primary influence comes from regional and supranational institutions rather than domestic actors.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains minimal at the national level, with compliance mechanisms largely dependent on EU framework implementation and voluntary adherence to Council of Europe standards.

Regulation Status: Angola follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and documentation rather than domestic legislation. The country has not enacted comprehensive national AI legislation as of early 2026, relying instead on regional protocols and voluntary guidelines promoted through AU channels.
Policy Lever: Angola engages with data and computing export controls through African Union coordination mechanisms. Policy implementation remains largely consultative and non-binding at the national level.
Governance Type: Angola adopts case-by-case and organizational governance focused on developing private sector engagement with human-centered AI principles. Governance structures emphasize multi-stakeholder dialogue rather than centralized regulatory bodies.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary external actors shaping Angola's AI policy direction through documentation and capacity-building initiatives. Angola participates in continental and international AI governance forums but maintains limited autonomous domestic regulatory infrastructure.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with reliance on voluntary compliance and organizational self-governance rather than formal penalties or audits.

Regulation Status: Antigua and Barbuda remains in early-stage AI regulation development with no binding AI-specific legislation enacted as of March 2026. The government has expressed interest in adopting EU-style AI regulation but has not yet formalized proposals into law or concrete policy frameworks.
Policy Lever: No formal policy mechanisms or regulatory levers have been implemented. The country relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks rather than AI-specific policy instruments.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structure or oversight body has been established. AI policy development appears to operate through general government channels without specialized institutional arrangements.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is limited to government-level considerations with minimal documented participation from private sector, academia, or civil society. Geographic scope remains domestic with no clear international coordination mechanisms visible.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation given the absence of binding legislation or designated regulatory authorities.

Regulation Status: Argentina maintains a non-binding, principles-based approach to AI governance through ethical guidelines rather than statutory regulation. As of early 2026, no comprehensive AI-specific legislation has been enacted, though the government continues to explore a light-touch regulatory framework aligned with its stated preference for innovation-friendly policies.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms consist primarily of voluntary ethical guidelines and stakeholder engagement rather than binding legal instruments. The government relies on industry self-regulation and international cooperation frameworks rather than prescriptive legislation.
Governance Type: Governance is decentralized and multi-stakeholder, involving coordination between government agencies, academic institutions, and industry players without a dedicated centralized AI regulatory authority. Decision-making reflects a preference for industry consultation and consensus-building over top-down regulation.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the Argentine national government, technology sector representatives, and academic institutions, with engagement at the national level. International participation occurs through MERCOSUR coordination and engagement with global AI governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist for AI governance; compliance with guidelines is voluntary and lacks penalty provisions or dedicated oversight bodies.

Regulation Status: Armenia has no domestically enacted national AI regulation as of March 2026, but participates in EU-aligned frameworks and Council of Europe initiatives. The country is following a risk-based approach influenced by EU standards and the Council of Europe's AI Convention adopted in March 2024.
Policy Lever: Armenia leverages data protection, human rights, democracy, and rule of law principles as policy mechanisms to address AI risks. The approach emphasizes institutional safeguards and future-risk mitigation through international framework alignment.
Governance Type: Governance structure remains primarily organizational and model-focused, with limited domestic institutional capacity. Oversight is largely delegated to regional and intergovernmental bodies through EU and Council of Europe frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Armenia engages with EU legal frameworks and Council of Europe mechanisms at the regional and intergovernmental level, with limited domestic governmental actors developing independent AI policy. International organizations play the primary role in shaping Armenian AI governance.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, as Armenia lacks dedicated domestic AI regulatory bodies or statutory enforcement authorities for AI-specific rules.

Regulation Status: Australia maintains a voluntary guardrails approach for high-risk AI applications without dedicated AI-specific legislation as of March 2026. Regulation is primarily addressed through existing frameworks in privacy, online safety, and consumer protection, supplemented by non-binding ethical guidelines at federal and state levels.
Policy Lever: Primary levers are voluntary industry guardrails, ethical frameworks, and incidental regulation through sector-specific laws rather than direct AI mandates. The approach relies on industry self-regulation and compliance with existing legal frameworks adapted to AI contexts.
Governance Type: Governance is fragmented across federal and state levels with no dedicated AI regulatory body. Multiple agencies address AI indirectly through privacy (OAIC), online safety (eSafety Commissioner), and consumer protection frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the federal government, state governments (particularly NSW with its assurance framework), industry bodies, and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner. Involvement spans national level policy with state-level implementation and private sector participation in voluntary schemes.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is limited to existing regulatory bodies applying general laws; no dedicated AI enforcement mechanism exists, resulting in soft compliance monitoring through voluntary guardrails and indirect sector-specific oversight.
Specific Laws: Privacy Act 1988, Online Safety Act 2021, Australian Consumer Law

Regulation Status: Austria operates under the EU AI Act (effective August 2024), implementing a risk-based regulatory framework that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk with corresponding compliance requirements. National implementation of the EU framework is ongoing, with Austria actively transposing directives and developing supplementary guidance for sectoral application.
Policy Lever: Austria employs data protection, human rights, and democratic governance principles as core policy levers, leveraging GDPR compliance mechanisms and fundamental rights assessments to mitigate AI risks. The policy framework emphasizes institutional oversight and algorithmic accountability across both public and private sectors.
Governance Type: Austria utilizes a multi-layered governance structure combining organizational compliance requirements with model governance standards, enforced through designated AI offices and data protection authorities. Governance includes impact assessments, algorithmic auditing, and transparency mechanisms embedded in organizational processes.
Actor Involvement: Regulation is coordinated at the EU regional level through the AI Act framework, with Austria's national implementation involving federal ministries, state authorities, and private sector stakeholders. International coordination occurs through EU institutions, with Austria participating in broader OECD and UN AI governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement is conducted through Austria's data protection authority (DSB), sectoral regulators, and EU-level oversight mechanisms, with administrative fines up to 6% of global revenue for AI Act violations and active audit and monitoring programs.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), GDPR (2018), Austrian Data Protection Act (2018), AI Liability Directive (EU, draft as of 2026)

Regulation Status: Azerbaijan follows Council of Europe AI framework guidelines through its membership in the Committee on Artificial Intelligence and has ratified the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI and human rights, but lacks comprehensive domestic AI legislation. The country adopts a compliance-based approach aligned with international standards rather than developing independent regulatory frameworks.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are primarily through international treaty alignment and voluntary adherence to Council of Europe recommendations. Domestic policy implementation relies on existing data protection and technology governance structures rather than AI-specific regulatory instruments.
Governance Type: Governance follows a centralized model through government agencies coordinating with international bodies, but lacks dedicated AI governance institutions. Responsibility is distributed across existing technology, data protection, and digital development ministries without specialized AI oversight bodies.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the Government of Azerbaijan, Council of Europe coordination bodies, and government technology agencies. Limited documented involvement of private sector, academia, or civil society in AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal with reliance on international compliance monitoring rather than active domestic enforcement bodies for AI-specific regulations.

Regulation Status: The Bahamas remains in early stages of AI regulation development with no comprehensive AI-specific legislation enacted as of March 2026. The government has been exploring policy frameworks and participating in international discussions on AI governance, but formal regulatory proposals are still under development.
Policy Lever: The Bahamas has relied primarily on voluntary guidelines and participation in regional initiatives rather than binding regulatory mechanisms. Policy development is ongoing through consultation with regional Caribbean bodies and international organizations.
Governance Type: Governance structure remains informal with responsibility scattered across multiple agencies including the Ministry of Technology and the Central Bank, without a dedicated AI regulatory body or clear institutional mandate.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is limited to government agencies and some participation in Caribbean Community (CARICOM) discussions on digital governance. Private sector and civil society engagement in formal AI policy development remains minimal.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as binding AI-specific rules have not been established.

Regulation Status: Bahrain has enacted a comprehensive risk-based AI regulatory framework as of April 2024, establishing binding rules for AI systems with potential societal impact. The framework applies to both public and private sector AI deployments with particular focus on high-risk applications.
Policy Lever: Bahrain employs specific prohibitions on AI systems that could incite societal unrest or political disturbance, with criminal penalties and extended liability to organizations deploying such systems. Regulations also target biometric tampering, privacy violations, and discriminatory AI applications.
Governance Type: Governance is structured through case-by-case organizational oversight with national-level bureaucratic coordination. Designated authorities evaluate AI systems for compliance with prohibitions on political destabilization and evaluate specific harms including biometric security, privacy invasion, and discrimination.
Actor Involvement: Implementation involves national government agencies and organizational-level compliance officers across Bahrain's public and private sectors. Responsibility extends to both system developers and deploying establishments under extended liability provisions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement includes criminal penalties for violations and organizational liability mechanisms, though effectiveness and consistency of enforcement across the private sector remains moderately developed.
Specific Laws: Bahrain Comprehensive AI Law (April 2024)

Regulation Status: Bangladesh has not ratified comprehensive AI regulation as of March 2026, maintaining a largely permissive approach with sector-specific guidelines emerging. The country continues to develop AI governance frameworks within existing regulatory structures rather than through dedicated AI legislation.
Policy Lever: Bangladesh employs data-driven policy making as a primary mechanism, with emphasis on AI applications in agriculture, medical science, climate change mitigation, and job creation. The government integrates AI considerations into sectoral strategies rather than standalone AI policy.
Governance Type: Case-based governance structure with specific focus on beneficial AI applications (agriculture, medical science, space exploration, climate change) while maintaining restrictions on weaponry applications. Governance remains distributed across sectoral ministries rather than centralized under a single AI authority.
Actor Involvement: Multiple stakeholders including ministries, academia, and private industries participate in AI governance, with supranational engagement through international conferences (latest April 2024). Involvement remains collaborative but not yet formalized through binding multilateral agreements.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are nascent with limited oversight infrastructure, relying primarily on sector-specific regulatory bodies rather than dedicated AI enforcement agencies.

Regulation Status: Barbados is in the early stages of AI engagement with no comprehensive AI-specific legislation enacted as of March 2026. The country has been developing proposals and participating in regional discussions but has not yet implemented formal AI regulatory frameworks. Existing governance relies on general data protection and consumer protection laws rather than AI-targeted regulation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are primarily voluntary and advisory, with engagement through regional Caribbean bodies and international frameworks rather than domestic binding legislation. The government has expressed interest in AI governance alignment with CARICOM initiatives but lacks specific domestic policy levers.
Governance Type: Governance is fragmented across general regulatory bodies (data protection authority, consumer affairs ministry) without a dedicated AI governance structure. Regional coordination through CARICOM provides some framework but national implementation remains nascent.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is limited to government ministries (particularly technology/digital transformation and consumer affairs) with minimal private sector or academic institution integration into formal regulatory processes. International engagement occurs through regional Caribbean organizations and selective participation in global AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated AI enforcement mechanisms exist; enforcement relies on general consumer protection and data privacy laws which have limited application to AI-specific harms.

Regulation Status: Belarus has minimal AI-specific regulation as of 2026, with no comprehensive national AI law enacted. The country has not adopted the EU AI Act framework and operates primarily through general data protection and technology governance mechanisms rather than risk-based AI-specific legislation.
Policy Lever: Belarus relies on general data protection regulations and sectoral oversight rather than dedicated AI policy mechanisms. Limited focus on human rights or democratic safeguards in AI governance compared to international standards.
Governance Type: AI governance in Belarus is fragmented across multiple government agencies without a dedicated central AI authority. Responsibility is distributed among sectoral regulators rather than consolidated organizational governance structures.
Actor Involvement: Belarus operates largely independently of EU regulatory frameworks and international AI governance initiatives, with limited participation in multilateral AI policy coordination. Governance is primarily domestic with minimal intergovernmental AI policy engagement.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms specific to AI systems are absent; oversight relies on general sectoral regulators with limited capacity or mandate for AI-specific compliance monitoring.

Regulation Status: Belgium operates under the EU AI Act (2024), a comprehensive risk-based regulatory framework categorizing AI systems from prohibited to high-risk and transparency-required systems. National implementation through federal and regional authorities is underway, with Belgium actively transposing EU requirements into domestic legal frameworks.
Policy Lever: Belgium employs data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic accountability mechanisms as primary policy levers, particularly through GDPR enforcement and AI Act compliance. The approach emphasizes institutional governance and risk mitigation through regulatory oversight.
Governance Type: Belgium utilizes multi-level governance combining EU regulatory frameworks with national implementation through federal and regional authorities, including the Data Protection Authority and sector-specific regulators. Model governance focuses on organizational compliance and risk management structures.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include EU institutions (European Commission, Parliament), Belgian federal and regional governments, the Belgian Data Protection Authority, and sectoral regulators operating within the EU AI Act framework. Governance operates at both regional/intergovernmental and national organizational levels.
Enforcement Level: The Belgian Data Protection Authority and designated national competent authorities conduct oversight with enforcement powers including inspections, audits, and administrative penalties aligned with EU AI Act provisions; enforcement intensity is increasing as implementation matures.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), Belgian Data Protection Act, General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) 2016/679, AI Liability Directive (under implementation)

Regulation Status: Belize remains in the early stages of AI regulation development with no comprehensive national AI policy or legislation enacted as of March 2026. The country has not yet formalized dedicated AI regulatory frameworks, though discussions regarding digital governance and technology policy continue at governmental and advisory levels.
Policy Lever: No specific policy mechanisms targeting AI have been formally implemented. Belize relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks rather than AI-specific regulatory levers.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structure exists in Belize. AI oversight remains fragmented across general telecommunications and digital economy portfolios without specialized institutional arrangements.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is limited to government ministries responsible for telecommunications and digital affairs, with minimal participation from private sector or international AI governance bodies. Regional Caribbean coordination on AI policy remains underdeveloped.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms or oversight bodies exist to monitor or penalize non-compliance with AI standards.

Regulation Status: Benin follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international collaboration rather than domestic legislation. The country lacks comprehensive national AI legislation but participates in AU-led initiatives promoting responsible AI development.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls within the African Union framework, with emphasis on capacity building and private sector engagement rather than restrictive regulatory measures.
Governance Type: Governance follows a case-by-case and organizational approach emphasizing private sector development and human-centered principles through international partnerships. Institutional structures for AI governance remain nascent with limited dedicated domestic oversight bodies.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the African Union (policy framework development), UNDP (capacity building), and G7 (international coordination), with Benin positioned as a participating member rather than a primary policy driver. Engagement is primarily at the continental and multilateral level rather than through strong bilateral relationships.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no established domestic enforcement bodies or penalty structures specific to AI regulation in Benin.

Regulation Status: Bhutan lacks comprehensive AI-specific legislation as of March 2026. The country is in early stages of AI engagement, with focus on digital transformation and ICT policy rather than dedicated AI regulation. AI governance remains largely absent from formal regulatory frameworks.
Policy Lever: Bhutan relies on general ICT policies and data protection frameworks rather than AI-specific mechanisms. The country has not yet established dedicated policy instruments targeting AI development, deployment, or safety.
Governance Type: Governance of AI matters falls under the Ministry of Information and Communications and broader digital governance structures. No specialized AI governance body or inter-ministerial committee for AI oversight has been established.
Actor Involvement: Limited involvement of government agencies, private sector, and international partners in formal AI governance. Bhutan participates in regional digital initiatives but has not prioritized AI policy development at the national level.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-related activities as formal AI regulation has not been enacted.

Regulation Status: Bolivia lacks dedicated national AI legislation as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on participation in regional frameworks and international declarations, with minimal domestic regulatory infrastructure for AI governance.
Policy Lever: Bolivia's AI policy approach is primarily data-focused through regional coordination mechanisms. The country has engaged with the Santiago Declaration framework but has not implemented binding national data protection or AI-specific legislative measures.
Governance Type: Governance is decentralized and informal, operating through regional organizational structures rather than dedicated national AI governance bodies. Bolivia participates in regional initiatives but lacks a formal national AI regulatory authority.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include participation in the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and commitment to the Santiago Declaration (2023). Involvement remains primarily at the regional association level with limited domestic stakeholder integration.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist; compliance is largely voluntary through regional framework participation without binding domestic enforcement structures.

Regulation Status: Bosnia and Herzegovina applies the EU AI Act through its EU integration framework, implementing risk-based regulation of AI systems across prohibited, high-risk, and transparency-obligation categories. The country is also subject to the EU's draft AI liability directive as it progresses toward full EU membership.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on human rights protection, democratic governance, and rule of law through data-based safeguards and institutional oversight. The country leverages EU regulatory frameworks to mitigate AI-related risks to fundamental rights and democratic processes.
Governance Type: Governance combines organizational structures with AI model governance, integrating national institutions with EU-level oversight bodies. Bosnia and Herzegovina implements both organizational compliance requirements and technical model governance standards derived from EU directives.
Actor Involvement: Governance operates at multiple levels: EU regulations provide the primary framework, intergovernmental coordination through EU institutions, and national implementation by Bosnian authorities. Regional actors include EU member states and intergovernmental bodies overseeing harmonized AI regulation.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms include EU oversight bodies and national competent authorities with investigatory powers, though enforcement capacity varies across Bosnia and Herzegovina's fragmented institutional structure.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), EU AI Liability Directive (draft as of 2026)

Regulation Status: Botswana is following a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. The country is in early stages of developing its own AI policy framework, relying heavily on continental guidance documents and voluntary guidelines.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are being considered as policy mechanisms, but implementation remains limited and largely aligned with African Union recommendations rather than independently enacted controls. Botswana has not yet established binding national export control mechanisms specific to AI or computing resources.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily case-based and organizational, focused on developing private sector engagement and promoting human-centered AI principles through collaboration with international bodies. Institutional frameworks for comprehensive AI governance remain underdeveloped at the national level.
Actor Involvement: Strong involvement from African Union, G7 through multilateral engagement, and UNDP technical assistance drives AI policy discussions in Botswana. Regional coordination through AU frameworks is significant, though direct G7 influence is indirect through international cooperation mechanisms.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with oversight primarily occurring through voluntary adherence to African Union guidelines and international best practices rather than binding national enforcement structures.

Regulation Status: Brazil continues developing AI regulation through draft legislation and sectoral policies. The National AI Working Group established in 2024 guides regulatory development, while existing data protection frameworks (LGPD) are being applied to AI contexts through enforcement actions and policy guidance.
Policy Lever: Brazil employs data protection and privacy as primary policy mechanisms, with sectoral approaches (elections, consumer protection) and organizational security requirements. Regulatory focus centers on data governance, transparency, and algorithmic accountability within existing LGPD frameworks.
Governance Type: Governance involves multiple actors including the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD), Consumer Secretariat, and an interministerial AI working group. Brazil combines sectoral regulation with cross-agency coordination to address AI impacts on elections, consumer protection, and data privacy.
Actor Involvement: Brazil engages in regional coordination (MERCOSUR context) and international advocacy for global AI governance standards, including G7 engagement. Domestic stakeholders include government agencies, private sector (particularly tech companies facing enforcement), civil society, and academic institutions.
Enforcement Level: Moderate enforcement through ANPD investigations and compliance orders (Meta cases), LGPD application to AI practices, and sectoral oversight; however, comprehensive AI-specific enforcement mechanisms remain limited pending final legislation.
Specific Laws: Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD, 2018), AI Electoral Rules (2024)

Regulation Status: Brunei lacks comprehensive AI-specific legislation as of March 2026, instead relying on ASEAN regional guidelines and general data protection frameworks. The regulatory approach remains largely principle-based and voluntary, with no binding national AI regulation formally ratified.
Policy Lever: Brunei's Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 serves as the primary policy mechanism, promoting AI innovation and digital transformation within a light-touch regulatory framework. The focus is on enabling business growth and technological advancement rather than restrictive regulation.
Governance Type: Governance is structured around institutional reforms supporting economic prosperity through technology leverage, with coordination between government ministries and private sector stakeholders. Decision-making remains centralized within the sultanate's governance structure with emphasis on case-by-case institutional adaptation.
Actor Involvement: Brunei engages primarily through ASEAN regional mechanisms and bilateral partnerships, participating in ASEAN AI governance discussions and initiatives rather than unilateral action. Domestic actors include government agencies, technology businesses, and research institutions aligned with regional standards.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with soft oversight through existing data protection and consumer protection bodies rather than dedicated AI compliance agencies or active penalty mechanisms.
Specific Laws: Personal Data Protection Act 2001, ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics 2024

Regulation Status: Bulgaria applies the EU AI Act (2024) as the primary risk-based regulatory framework, categorizing AI systems by risk level with prohibitions on high-risk applications and transparency requirements for lower-risk systems. The country operates within the EU's harmonized approach while transposing regulations into national law and preparing for the AI Liability Directive implementation.
Policy Lever: Bulgaria leverages data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic principles through GDPR alignment and AI Act compliance mechanisms. The regulatory approach emphasizes protecting rule of law and democratic institutions through institutional oversight and transparency obligations.
Governance Type: Bulgaria employs organizational governance structures through designated competent authorities and model governance via technical standards compliance. Governance integrates EU-level institutional frameworks with national implementation bodies.
Actor Involvement: EU-level governance dominates through the European Commission and standardization bodies (ETSI, CEN, CENELEC), while Bulgaria coordinates through national authorities and participates in intergovernmental mechanisms. International cooperation extends through OECD AI principles and multilateral AI governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act provides enforcement mechanisms with administrative fines up to €30 million or 6% of global turnover, administered through national authorities and supported by periodic audits and compliance monitoring.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), GDPR (2018), Draft AI Liability Directive (2022), Bulgarian Law on Protection of Personal Data (2018)

Regulation Status: Burkina Faso has not enacted domesticated AI-specific legislation as of March 2026. The country participates in African Union-led human rights-centered AI governance initiatives and regional policy frameworks, but lacks comprehensive national AI regulation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms remain largely aspirational, focusing on data governance and computing export controls through African Union coordination rather than enforceable national measures. Implementation of these levers is minimal at the domestic level.
Governance Type: Burkina Faso follows a developing case-based and organizational governance model emphasizing private sector engagement and human-centered approaches aligned with African Union directives. Governance structures remain nascent with limited institutional capacity for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Involvement includes the African Union, G7 coordination, and UNDP technical assistance in advancing AI policy frameworks. Regional and multilateral engagement exceeds domestic institutional capacity for independent AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No documented enforcement mechanisms or dedicated oversight bodies exist for AI regulation in Burkina Faso as of March 2026.

Regulation Status: Burundi follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. The country has not yet enacted dedicated national AI laws but engages with AU-promoted blueprints and resolutions on AI governance.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are identified as a policy mechanism, though implementation remains limited. Burundi relies on regional African Union guidance rather than independent domestic policy levers.
Governance Type: Governance follows a case-by-case and organizational approach with emphasis on developing private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Institutional capacity for AI-specific governance remains nascent.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary actors shaping AI policy frameworks relevant to Burundi. Burundi's own governmental agencies have limited direct involvement in AI regulation setting compared to international actors.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation in Burundi; compliance with AU guidelines is voluntary and lacks direct enforcement infrastructure.

Regulation Status: Cambodia follows ASEAN's AI governance framework but has not ratified independent national AI legislation as of March 2026. The country relies on regional guidelines and sectoral regulations rather than comprehensive AI-specific laws.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms consist primarily of voluntary ASEAN guidelines and organizational frameworks rather than binding legislative instruments. Limited sector-specific policies exist for telecommunications and data protection.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational with intraregional coordination through ASEAN structures, including relevant ministries and technical working groups. Domestic implementation relies on existing institutions without dedicated AI regulatory bodies.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include ASEAN member states through regional coordination mechanisms, with involvement from Cambodia's Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications and relevant sectoral agencies. International engagement occurs through ASEAN frameworks rather than bilateral AI agreements.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with reliance on voluntary compliance with ASEAN guidelines and existing sectoral regulators lacking specific AI enforcement mandates.
Specific Laws: Law on Telecommunications (2009), Personal Data Protection Law (2021)

Regulation Status: Cameroon has adopted a human rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through participation in African Union initiatives rather than domestic legislation. The country lacks comprehensive national AI legislation but engages with continental frameworks and guidelines developed through AU, G7, and UNDP partnerships.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls serve as the primary policy mechanism, though implementation remains limited. Cameroon participates in broader African frameworks addressing data sovereignty and digital infrastructure rather than having standalone export control mechanisms.
Governance Type: Governance relies on case-by-case and organizational approaches with emphasis on private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Institutional structures remain nascent without dedicated national AI governance bodies or established regulatory agencies.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary external actors shaping AI policy direction in Cameroon through collaborative initiatives and technical support. Domestic private sector involvement is being developed but remains limited in scope.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist; compliance with AI governance principles remains voluntary and largely aspirational without penalties or systematic oversight bodies.

Regulation Status: Canada is pursuing a risk-based regulatory approach with draft AI legislation under development and ongoing multi-sector consultations. The government has not yet enacted comprehensive binding AI legislation but has established a framework for responsible AI use at the national level.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms include sector-specific consultations addressing copyright, privacy, and competition concerns, combined with principles-based guidance on responsible AI use. The government is leveraging consultation processes to inform legislative design across multiple policy domains.
Governance Type: Canada is implementing model governance through an AI government initiative with centralized coordination at the national level. Governance structures remain under development as part of the legislative consultation process.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is currently concentrated at the national federal level with limited evidence of provincial or municipal governance integration. International coordination mechanisms are not prominently featured in available information.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal given the draft status of legislation; current activity focuses on consultation and policy development rather than active enforcement of binding rules.

Regulation Status: Cape Verde adopts a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through participation in African Union frameworks and international initiatives rather than domestic legislation. The country engages with AU blueprints and resolutions on AI governance but lacks comprehensive national AI-specific laws.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union coordination. Implementation remains limited, with emphasis on establishing frameworks rather than active enforcement of controls.
Governance Type: Cape Verde employs case-by-case and organizational governance with emphasis on developing private sector engagement and human-centered AI approaches. Governance structure remains nascent with limited institutional capacity for comprehensive AI regulation.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the African Union (regional framework), G7 (through international cooperation), and UNDP (capacity building). Cape Verde participates in AU-led AI governance initiatives while maintaining limited bilateral engagement with G7 mechanisms.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated national AI regulatory body or active penalty structures for AI-related violations.

Regulation Status: The Central African Republic follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. National AI policy development is nascent, with the country relying on continental guidelines and UNDP-supported capacity building initiatives.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are promoted at the continental level through AU frameworks, but domestic implementation mechanisms in the CAR remain underdeveloped. Policy levers focus on alignment with African Union directives rather than independent national instruments.
Governance Type: Governance adopts a case-by-case and organizational approach centered on developing private sector engagement and human-centered principles. The CAR lacks a dedicated national AI regulatory body, instead relying on ad-hoc coordination through international partnerships.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the African Union, G7 coordination mechanisms, and UNDP technical support. The CAR government participates in continental initiatives but has limited independent regulatory capacity or bilateral AI agreements.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated CAR institutional capacity for AI regulation oversight or compliance monitoring.

Regulation Status: Chad follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. The country has not yet enacted standalone AI-specific laws but participates in continental initiatives promoting responsible AI development.
Policy Lever: Chad relies on data and computing export controls as a policy mechanism, implemented through regional African Union coordination. These controls remain largely informal and voluntary without robust domestic enforcement mechanisms.
Governance Type: Chad employs a case-by-case and organizational governance approach focused on developing private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Governance structures remain nascent with limited institutional capacity for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the African Union, G7 through technical cooperation, and UNDP providing development support. Chad's direct participation in international AI governance forums is limited compared to more developed nations.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal with no dedicated national AI regulatory body or active enforcement of AI-specific rules.

Regulation Status: Chile has a draft AI bill in progress as of May 2024, following a risk-based approach aligned with UNESCO recommendations. The country has issued interpretative circulars on AI and consumer protection, indicating movement toward formal regulation while legislation remains under development.
Policy Lever: Chile employs data-driven and computing policy mechanisms with emphasis on robotics development. Policy levers include national AI strategy initiatives and incentive mechanisms to support AI innovation alongside regulatory frameworks.
Governance Type: Governance operates through organizational structures and interpretative circulars, with the 2023 Circular on Consumer Protection Against Artificial Intelligence Systems providing case-by-case guidance. A mixed approach combining regulatory guidance with evolving institutional frameworks is evident.
Actor Involvement: Chile participates actively in regional AI governance through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean, having signed the 2023 Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI. National actors coordinate with regional bodies while developing domestic policy frameworks.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain limited, relying primarily on interpretative circulars and consumer protection guidance rather than statutory penalties, with formal enforcement anticipated pending completion of the draft AI bill.
Specific Laws: Circular on Consumer Protection Against the use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (2023), Draft AI Bill (introduced May 2024)

Regulation Status: China has implemented active regulation of AI with multiple sector-specific laws and guidelines, focusing on generative AI, algorithmic recommendations, and data security. The regulatory framework combines top-down governmental mandates with organizational-level compliance requirements, evolving from initial guidelines (2017-2021) to binding legislation (2022-2026).
Policy Lever: China employs comprehensive policy mechanisms including data governance, algorithm transparency and control, content security requirements, and model training restrictions. Regulatory levers target data flows, algorithmic decision-making processes, generative AI model safety, and cross-border data transfers through multiple coordinated agencies.
Governance Type: Governance combines central government directives with organizational compliance frameworks, involving CAC (Cyberspace Administration), MIIT, and sector regulators in coordinated oversight. Deep synthesis approach integrates technology requirements, generative AI safeguards, and case-by-case model review mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Multi-level actors include central government agencies (CAC, MIIT, NDRC), provincial authorities, industry organizations, and technology companies in hierarchical compliance structure. International engagement through BRICS and bilateral arrangements complements national top-down regulatory approach.
Enforcement Level: Active enforcement through CAC audits, administrative penalties, business license revocation, financial fines, and content takedown orders; documented enforcement actions against non-compliant platforms increased significantly 2023-2026.
Specific Laws: Generative AI Service Management Regulations (2023), Algorithm Recommendation Service Governance Regulations (2022), Data Security Law (2021), Personal Information Protection Law (2021), Online Content Security Assessment Guidelines (2020), Network Security Law (2016 amended 2021)

Regulation Status: Colombia lacks comprehensive AI-specific legislation as of March 2026, operating primarily under voluntary guidelines and sectoral regulations. The country participates in regional AI governance frameworks through the Santiago Declaration but has not enacted binding national AI laws.
Policy Lever: Colombia relies on data protection frameworks (particularly GDPR-influenced approaches) and sectoral regulations rather than dedicated AI policy levers. Regional commitments through the Intergovernmental Council on AI provide soft governance mechanisms.
Governance Type: Governance operates through a combination of organizational frameworks and regional intergovernmental coordination, with limited dedicated national AI governance structures. Multiple sectoral ministries address AI tangentially without centralized coordination.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the regional Intergovernmental Council on AI for Latin America and the Caribbean, Colombian government ministries, and private sector participation. Engagement remains primarily at regional rather than national dedicated levels.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated AI regulatory body or active compliance monitoring for AI-specific rules in Colombia.

Regulation Status: Comoros follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and regional initiatives rather than domestic legislation. The country has not enacted specific national AI laws but participates in continental AI policy development efforts.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union initiatives. Implementation at the national level remains limited with reliance on regional coordination rather than autonomous domestic policy instruments.
Governance Type: Governance follows a case-by-case and organizational approach emphasizing private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Institutional capacity for AI governance remains underdeveloped with limited dedicated agencies or structures.
Actor Involvement: Comoros participates through African Union leadership, with support from G7 and UNDP engagement on AI policy. However, direct national institutional involvement and capacity remain modest compared to larger African nations.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms specific to AI regulation exist at the national level, with soft enforcement relying primarily on voluntary compliance through regional frameworks.

Regulation Status: Congo follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. The country lacks comprehensive national AI regulation but participates in continental initiatives promoting responsible AI development.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union coordination. These are largely aspirational and coordinated through international agreements rather than enforced through domestic legislation.
Governance Type: Governance operates through case-by-case and organizational approaches emphasizing private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Congo lacks a dedicated national AI governance authority or formal institutional structure for AI regulation.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP play significant roles in shaping Congo's AI policy direction through documentation and collaborative initiatives. Congo's direct institutional capacity and independent policy-making mechanisms remain limited.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated domestic agency actively enforcing AI-related regulations or standards.

Regulation Status: Costa Rica remains in the draft legislation phase with the Law Regulating Artificial Intelligence still under development as of 2026. The country has adopted regional frameworks including the Santiago Declaration and the Ibero-American Charter on AI in Public Administration, but has not yet enacted binding national AI legislation.
Policy Lever: Costa Rica's approach focuses primarily on data governance and public administration oversight through regional frameworks. Policy mechanisms remain largely non-binding and aspirational rather than enforced through specific legislative instruments.
Governance Type: Governance is organizationally structured through participation in regional bodies like the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean. Domestic governance structures for AI oversight have not yet been formally established through enacted legislation.
Actor Involvement: Costa Rica actively engages through regional associations and multilateral frameworks, particularly via the IACAC and Ibero-American mechanisms. International cooperation is prioritized, though domestic institutional actors and enforcement bodies remain underdeveloped.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist as legislation remains in draft form; compliance with regional declarations is voluntary and lacks binding enforcement power.

Regulation Status: Croatia applies the EU AI Act as a member state, implementing a risk-based regulatory framework that categorizes AI systems as prohibited, high-risk, or subject to transparency obligations. The country has transposed EU directives into national law and is developing complementary national AI governance mechanisms aligned with EU standards.
Policy Lever: Croatia leverages data protection, human rights, and rule of law principles as policy mechanisms, with emphasis on institutional capacity building and mitigating systemic risks through governance frameworks. The approach integrates democratic safeguards and transparency requirements into AI system oversight.
Governance Type: Croatia employs a hybrid governance model combining organizational oversight with AI model governance requirements, implementing both entity-level compliance and technical model evaluation standards. The structure incorporates designated competent authorities responsible for AI Act enforcement and sectoral regulators.
Actor Involvement: Croatia's AI regulation operates within the EU regulatory framework at the supranational level, with the European Commission, national data protection authority, and designated national competent authorities as primary actors. Implementation involves coordination across government agencies, sectoral regulators, and participation in EU-level AI governance bodies.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through designated national competent authorities with powers to conduct audits, issue compliance notices, and impose penalties aligned with EU AI Act provisions, though full implementation capacity continues to develop.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, GDPR (2018), eIDAS Regulation (2014)

Regulation Status: Cuba has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of March 2026. The country maintains minimal formal regulatory frameworks specific to artificial intelligence, relying instead on broader data governance and technology policies under state control.
Policy Lever: Cuba's approach centers on data governance and state oversight mechanisms rather than specific AI regulation. The country participates in regional declarations but has not translated these into binding domestic policy instruments.
Governance Type: Governance remains primarily organizational and state-directed through existing telecommunications and information technology authorities rather than dedicated AI governance bodies. Cuba's centralized administrative structure shapes policy implementation without specialized AI regulatory institutions.
Actor Involvement: Cuba participates in regional frameworks through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and has signed the Santiago Declaration (2023) on ethical AI. Limited engagement with international AI governance beyond regional Latin American mechanisms.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-specific regulations, though general data and technology policies are enforced through state administrative structures.

Regulation Status: Cyprus implements the EU AI Act (2024) as its primary AI-specific regulatory framework, establishing a risk-based approach that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk to transparency-compliant. The country is also subject to the AI Liability Directive (2024), which establishes fault-based and strict liability regimes for AI-caused damages. Cyprus has transposed EU AI regulations into national law through implementing decrees and administrative guidelines.
Policy Lever: Cyprus leverages data protection, human rights frameworks, and rule of law principles as core policy mechanisms, integrated through the GDPR and EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. The policy approach emphasizes institutional capacity-building and democratic safeguards through data governance structures aligned with EU standards.
Governance Type: Cyprus employs multi-level governance combining organizational compliance mechanisms with algorithmic governance oversight. The Office of the Commissioner for Personal Data Protection serves as the primary national competent authority, supported by sectoral regulators and institutional coordination bodies aligned with EU governance structures.
Actor Involvement: Governance involves EU institutions (European Commission, Parliament, Council), national authorities (Cyprus government, data protection commissioner, sectoral regulators), and intergovernmental coordination through the EU AI Board. Cyprus participates in EU-wide enforcement and policy harmonization mechanisms.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act establishes binding enforcement through national competent authorities with powers to conduct audits, issue compliance notices, and impose fines up to €30 million or 6% of global revenue for prohibited systems violations.
Specific Laws: EU Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (AI Act) 2024, EU Directive (EU) 2024/1689 (AI Liability Directive) 2024, General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) 2016, Cyprus Data Protection Law (as amended for EU compliance) 2018

Regulation Status: Czechia implements the EU AI Act (2024) as part of the broader EU regulatory framework, establishing a risk-based approach that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk with varying compliance requirements. The country operates within the EU's harmonized legal framework while developing complementary national implementation measures.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms center on human rights protection, democratic safeguards, and rule of law through data governance and AI risk mitigation strategies. Czech authorities emphasize institutional capacity-building and alignment with EU standards for trustworthy AI development.
Governance Type: Governance combines organizational and algorithmic model oversight through a multi-level structure involving national authorities, EU regulatory bodies, and sectoral supervisors. The Czech Republic implements EU-level governance directives while establishing national coordination mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Multiple actors operate across EU and national levels, including the European Commission, national data protection authorities, sectoral regulators, and Czech government bodies responsible for AI implementation. International coordination occurs through EU institutions and intergovernmental frameworks.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through EU-coordinated mechanisms with national implementation, including oversight bodies, compliance audits, and penalties for non-compliance with the AI Act, though intensity varies by sector.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), EU AI Liability Directive (draft/under finalization)

Regulation Status: Côte d'Ivoire lacks dedicated national AI legislation as of March 2026, instead relying on a human-rights-centered approach promoted through African Union frameworks and international partnerships. The country is engaged in developing AI governance principles aligned with continental initiatives rather than implementing binding domestic AI-specific laws.
Policy Lever: Primary policy mechanisms focus on data governance and computing export controls within the broader African Union framework, with limited implementation of concrete national mechanisms. The approach emphasizes capacity-building and private sector engagement rather than restrictive export control measures.
Governance Type: Governance operates through case-by-case and organizational approaches, prioritizing private sector development and human-centered AI principles aligned with African Union guidelines. Structures remain largely informal without established dedicated AI regulatory agencies or dedicated governance bodies.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP play significant roles in shaping Côte d'Ivoire's AI governance approach through capacity-building and policy guidance. Engagement is primarily at the continental and international level rather than through bilateral arrangements.
Enforcement Level: No established enforcement mechanisms for AI-specific regulations exist; oversight remains minimal and relies primarily on existing general data protection and consumer protection frameworks.

Regulation Status: The DRC follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance largely through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. No DRC-specific AI regulation has been enacted, with governance occurring primarily through continental initiatives and soft policy coordination mechanisms.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are nominally promoted through AU documentation but lack DRC-specific implementation mechanisms. Policy remains largely aspirational without binding domestic enforcement structures.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily case-by-case and organizational, focusing on private sector engagement and human-centered principles through AU coordination. The DRC lacks a dedicated national AI governance body or institutional framework for systematic oversight.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7 (indirectly), and UNDP are primary actors shaping AI policy direction for the DRC through continental blueprints and development programs. Domestic institutional capacity for independent AI governance remains limited.
Enforcement Level: No active enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation in the DRC; oversight is primarily advisory through international partnerships without penalty structures or compliance monitoring.

Regulation Status: Denmark implements the EU AI Act (effective August 2024) as the primary regulatory framework for AI systems, applying a risk-based approach across all sectors. The country also participates in the EU's AI liability directive framework and maintains alignment with broader EU digital regulation standards.
Policy Lever: Denmark employs data governance, human rights impact assessments, and democratic safeguards through the EU AI Act implementation, complemented by national data protection measures under GDPR. The country emphasizes institutional oversight and transparency obligations for high-risk AI systems.
Governance Type: Governance is structured through organizational and algorithmic accountability mechanisms, with the Danish Data Protection Authority (Datatilsynet) serving as the primary national regulator. Denmark operates within the EU's coordinated framework while maintaining national implementation flexibility.
Actor Involvement: Denmark participates in EU-level governance through the European Commission and European Board of AI, while national implementation involves Datatilsynet, industry stakeholders, and civil society. International engagement includes OECD AI Policy Observatory membership and participation in international AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: The Danish Data Protection Authority actively enforces AI Act compliance through audits, investigations, and administrative penalties aligned with EU enforcement mechanisms, though comprehensive enforcement matured as implementation progressed through 2024-2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act 2024, GDPR 2018, Danish Data Protection Act 2018

Regulation Status: Djibouti has not enacted domestic AI-specific legislation as of March 2026. The country's AI governance approach is primarily shaped by participation in African Union frameworks promoting human-rights-centered AI principles rather than independent national regulation.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls remain theoretical policy levers for Djibouti, with limited implementation capacity. The country relies on AU guidelines and international frameworks rather than autonomous regulatory mechanisms.
Governance Type: Djibouti follows a case-by-case and emerging organizational governance model aligned with African Union human-centered AI principles. Private sector engagement remains minimal with limited institutional capacity for AI governance implementation.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, UNDP, and G7 influence Djibouti's AI policy direction through multilateral frameworks and capacity-building initiatives. Djibouti's own institutional actors have limited autonomous role in shaping AI regulation.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation in Djibouti, with compliance dependent on voluntary adoption of AU guidelines and international best practices.

Regulation Status: Dominica has no dedicated AI-specific legislation or national AI strategy as of March 2026. The country relies on participation in regional frameworks rather than domestic regulatory approaches.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are primarily regional and voluntary through the Santiago Declaration (2023) on ethical AI. No domestic policy levers have been formally implemented.
Governance Type: Governance is conducted through regional organizational structures via the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean. No domestic institutional framework for AI governance exists.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors are regional bodies through the Santiago Declaration mechanism. Dominica participates as a member of the regional council but lacks domestic actors or ministries dedicated to AI policy.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist at the national level; regional voluntary commitments lack binding enforcement provisions.

Regulation Status: Dominican Republic has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on participation in regional frameworks and general data protection principles rather than comprehensive national AI regulation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data governance and adherence to regional declarations, particularly the Santiago Declaration framework for ethical AI in Latin America and the Caribbean.
Governance Type: Governance operates at the organizational and regional level through participation in the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean, with limited domestic institutional structures dedicated to AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include regional associations and the Dominican government's participation in the Santiago Declaration framework; international cooperation through Latin American and Caribbean intergovernmental bodies provides the primary governance structure.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, relying on voluntary compliance with regional declarations rather than binding domestic legal penalties or active monitoring.

Regulation Status: East Timor has no dedicated AI regulation or national AI policy framework as of March 2026. The country lacks specific legislation governing artificial intelligence development, deployment, or use across sectors.
Policy Lever: No formal policy mechanisms or regulatory levers for AI governance have been established. East Timor relies on general ICT and technology oversight without AI-specific provisions.
Governance Type: There is no dedicated institutional structure or governance body responsible for AI regulation in East Timor. AI oversight, if any, falls under general technology and telecommunications authorities.
Actor Involvement: Limited stakeholder engagement in AI policy formulation; involvement is primarily ad-hoc through international development organizations and donor-supported technology initiatives. No evidence of domestic private sector or civil society coordinated participation in AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-specific regulations or compliance monitoring.

Regulation Status: Ecuador lacks comprehensive AI-specific legislation as of 2026. The country has engaged with regional AI governance frameworks through the Santiago Declaration (2023) but has not implemented domestic binding AI regulations or a national AI strategy.
Policy Lever: Ecuador's approach relies primarily on international cooperation and data governance frameworks rather than dedicated AI policy mechanisms. Engagement with regional declarations provides soft governance guidance without mandatory implementation requirements.
Governance Type: Governance remains largely organizational and informal, centered on participation in regional bodies like the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean. No dedicated national AI governance body or regulatory agency has been formally established.
Actor Involvement: Ecuador participates in regional AI governance through Latin American and Caribbean initiatives, particularly the Santiago Declaration framework. Involvement remains at the regional association level without substantial domestic institutional development.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms for AI regulation exist; compliance with regional declarations is voluntary and lacks enforcement infrastructure.

Regulation Status: Egypt has adopted a National AI Strategy (2021) with an AI ethics framework and charter for responsible AI, but lacks comprehensive AI-specific legislation. The Senate's Education Committee has approved a proposal for issuing a document on AI ethics and control, indicating movement toward formal regulation.
Policy Lever: Egypt uses strategic planning through its National AI Strategy with four pillars (government, development, capacity building, international relations) and four enablers (governance, data, ecosystem, infrastructure). Policy development is progressing through parliamentary committees working on ethics documentation and regulatory frameworks.
Governance Type: Egypt employs a coordinated governance model combining organizational structures with strategic frameworks across government, development, and capacity building sectors. Governance is structured around national pillars and enablers with multi-stakeholder coordination, though formal regulatory bodies for AI oversight remain under development.
Actor Involvement: Egypt's AI governance involves national-level actors including government ministries, the Senate Education Committee, and educational institutions, with explicit ambitions to become a regional AI power in the Middle East and Africa. International relations are identified as a strategic pillar supporting broader regional influence.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal; reliance on voluntary adherence to ethics frameworks and guidelines rather than statutory penalties, though institutional oversight structures are being established through parliamentary action.
Specific Laws: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI, National AI Strategy (2021)

Regulation Status: El Salvador has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of March 2026. The country's regulatory approach remains nascent, relying primarily on participation in regional frameworks and broad existing data protection laws rather than AI-specific statutory regulation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to data protection frameworks and voluntary adherence to regional declarations. The Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI serves as a non-binding aspirational guideline rather than enforceable regulation.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational and regional rather than national. El Salvador coordinates through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean, with minimal domestic institutional structure dedicated to AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the regional Intergovernmental Council on AI for Latin America and the Caribbean, with El Salvador's government participation in the Santiago Declaration (2023). Domestic private sector and civil society engagement in AI governance remains underdeveloped.
Enforcement Level: No specific enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, with compliance dependent on voluntary adoption of regional ethical principles rather than legal mandate or institutional oversight.

Regulation Status: Equatorial Guinea participates in African Union-led human-rights-centered AI governance initiatives through continental frameworks rather than domestic legislation. The country has not enacted specific national AI laws but is influenced by AU policy documentation including AI blueprints and resolutions.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are identified as potential policy mechanisms within continental frameworks. Implementation at the national level remains limited and largely theoretical rather than operationalized.
Governance Type: Governance approach emphasizes case-by-case and organizational governance with private sector engagement and human-centered principles derived from African Union guidance. Institutional capacity for AI governance remains nascent.
Actor Involvement: Engagement occurs primarily through African Union membership and participation in UNDP and G7 Africa-related initiatives. Direct bilateral AI governance relationships or agreements are not clearly established.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms for AI regulation have been established at the national level in Equatorial Guinea.

Regulation Status: Eritrea has minimal direct AI regulation, relying primarily on regional African Union frameworks and human-rights-centered governance principles promoted through AU documentation. The country lacks comprehensive national AI legislation or dedicated regulatory bodies for AI governance.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are identified as potential policy mechanisms, but implementation remains limited. Eritrea's approach is primarily shaped by broader African Union guidelines rather than independent national policy instruments.
Governance Type: Eritrea adopts case-by-case and organizational governance with emphasis on developing private sector participation and human-centered AI principles. Governance structures remain nascent with limited institutional capacity for AI-specific regulation.
Actor Involvement: Eritrea engages with the African Union's AI governance initiatives, with indirect involvement from UNDP and G7-influenced international frameworks. Regional coordination through AU mechanisms represents the primary external influence on national AI policy direction.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms or institutional capacity for AI regulation enforcement exists in Eritrea as of 2026.

Regulation Status: Estonia applies the EU AI Act (2024) as its primary AI-specific regulatory framework, implementing a risk-based approach that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk to general transparency obligations. The country has transposed EU requirements into national implementation while maintaining its digital-first governance model.
Policy Lever: Estonia leverages data governance, human rights safeguards, and democratic accountability as core policy mechanisms, supported by institutional frameworks for algorithmic transparency and accountability. The country integrates AI regulation with its broader digital governance strategy and rule of law initiatives.
Governance Type: Estonia employs both organizational governance through designated AI oversight bodies and model governance mechanisms aligned with EU standards. National implementation is coordinated through Estonia's digital government institutions and the Information System Authority (RIA).
Actor Involvement: Governance operates at multiple levels: EU framework setting (European Commission, Parliament), national implementation (Estonian government, RIA), and coordination with intergovernmental structures.
