Estonia participates in EU-wide AI regulation harmonization and international AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through national competent authorities implementing EU AI Act provisions, with the Information System Authority overseeing compliance, conducting audits, and applying administrative penalties for violations.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act 2024, Estonian implementation of AI Act (ongoing), AI Liability Directive (EU, in implementation)

Regulation Status: Ethiopia is developing a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. The country relies on regional guidelines and blueprints promoted by the AU, UNDP, and G7 engagement rather than independent national AI laws.
Policy Lever: Ethiopia's primary policy mechanism focuses on data and computing export controls within the African Union framework. Limited domestic policy levers are currently implemented, with emphasis on regional coordination through AU initiatives.
Governance Type: Governance structure emphasizes case-by-case and organizational approaches with nascent private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Ethiopia participates in AU-led multi-stakeholder governance models rather than maintaining independent institutional structures.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the African Union, G7, and UNDP, with Ethiopia positioned as a participating nation in regional initiatives. International cooperation dominates governance, with limited evidence of dedicated Ethiopian domestic institutions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, relying primarily on voluntary guidelines and regional AU coordination rather than domestic penalties or active oversight bodies.

Regulation Status: Fiji has not enacted dedicated AI-specific legislation or comprehensive regulatory frameworks for artificial intelligence as of March 2026. The country remains in an early stage with minimal formal AI governance structures in place.
Policy Lever: No formal policy levers or mechanisms specific to AI regulation have been established. Fiji relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks rather than AI-targeted policies.
Governance Type: Governance remains fragmented with potential oversight distributed across existing ICT and telecommunications authorities rather than a dedicated AI governance body. No centralized AI policy coordination mechanism appears to exist.
Actor Involvement: Limited involvement from government, private sector, or international partners in formal AI governance. Fiji participates in regional Pacific Island forums but has not led or prominently engaged in AI policy initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms or dedicated oversight bodies exist to monitor compliance with AI governance standards.

Regulation Status: Finland has implemented the EU AI Act (2024) as binding law and maintains a proactive national AI governance framework. The country combines EU-wide regulation with domestic initiatives through its AI strategy and supports a principles-based approach to emerging AI risks.
Policy Lever: Finland uses mandatory compliance mechanisms through EU AI Act implementation, sector-specific guidelines, sandboxes for innovation testing, and public procurement standards for AI systems. The government also employs risk-based classification and transparency requirements.
Governance Type: Multi-level governance involving the Finnish Ministry of Transport and Communications, the Office of the Data Protection Ombudsman, and sectoral regulators coordinating with EU bodies. Finland emphasizes participatory governance through stakeholder consultation and industry collaboration.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include government ministries, the data protection authority, research institutions, and private sector organizations. Finland actively participates in EU AI policy harmonization and maintains international cooperation through OECD and UN forums.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through the Data Protection Ombudsman, sector-specific regulators, and EU-coordinated compliance mechanisms with administrative penalties and sanctions for non-compliance with the AI Act.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act 2024, Finnish Data Protection Act 2018, Act on Algorithmic Decision-Making Transparency 2023

Regulation Status: France applies the EU AI Act (effective since 2024) as the primary binding regulatory framework, supplemented by national implementation measures and the emerging AI Liability Directive. The risk-based approach categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk to low-risk with corresponding obligations.
Policy Lever: France leverages data protection frameworks (GDPR), human rights safeguards, and institutional oversight to mitigate AI risks. The country emphasizes democratic accountability and rule of law through requirements for transparency, impact assessments, and algorithmic auditing.
Governance Type: Governance combines EU-level regulatory oversight with national implementation through the CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés) and sectoral regulators. Model governance includes requirements for documentation, testing, and continuous monitoring of high-risk AI systems.
Actor Involvement: The EU AI Act provides the primary legal framework with enforcement by EU member states including France, while CNIL serves as the national competent authority. International coordination occurs through EU institutions, with industry engagement through conformity assessments and certification procedures.
Enforcement Level: CNIL and sectoral authorities conduct audits and investigations with power to issue fines up to €30 million or 6% of global revenue for EU AI Act violations; enforcement began ramping up in 2025-2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2023, effective 2024), GDPR (2018), Draft AI Liability Directive (2022, ongoing), French National AI Strategy (2017 updated 2022)

Regulation Status: Gabon is following the African Union's human-rights-centered AI governance framework through regional documentation rather than enacting domestic legislation. The country participates in AU-led initiatives promoting responsible AI development but lacks comprehensive national AI legislation as of March 2026.
Policy Lever: Gabon relies primarily on data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union coordination mechanisms. These controls are part of emerging regional frameworks rather than established domestic policy enforcement.
Governance Type: Gabon employs a developing case-by-case and organizational governance approach focused on private sector engagement and human-centered principles. The framework emphasizes consultation with international partners rather than binding regulatory structures.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary actors shaping Gabon's AI governance through regional initiatives and capacity-building partnerships. Gabon's direct institutional involvement remains limited, with governance largely influenced by continental and multilateral frameworks.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with reliance on voluntary compliance and international coordination rather than domestic legal penalties or active auditing.

Regulation Status: Gambia has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of 2026. The country is engaged in regional AI governance frameworks promoted by the African Union, which emphasize human rights-centered approaches through various documentation including blueprints and whitepapers.
Policy Lever: Gambia relies primarily on data and computing export controls as a policy mechanism, aligned with regional African Union initiatives. No domestic AI-specific regulatory levers have been independently implemented.
Governance Type: Governance remains nascent with emphasis on developing private sector engagement and human-centered principles through case-by-case and organizational approaches. There is no dedicated national AI governance institution or comprehensive framework specific to Gambia.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are key external actors shaping AI governance direction through regional initiatives. Gambia participates in these multilateral frameworks but maintains limited autonomous policy-making capacity in AI regulation.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist in Gambia; enforcement remains informal through international partnership frameworks rather than domestic institutional capacity.

Regulation Status: Georgia is in the process of developing AI-specific regulation, primarily through EU alignment mechanisms as it pursues EU integration. The country applies the EU AI Act framework to some extent but lacks comprehensive domestic AI legislation as of March 2026.
Policy Lever: Georgia employs a risk-based approach aligned with EU frameworks, focusing on human rights, democracy, and rule of law considerations. Institutional development and data governance are key mechanisms for addressing AI risks.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational, with model-level oversight developing through EU alignment. Georgian authorities coordinate with international bodies and EU institutions rather than having fully independent domestic governance structures.
Actor Involvement: EU institutions, the Georgian government, and intergovernmental bodies play significant roles in shaping AI policy. Georgia's AI regulation is heavily influenced by EU frameworks due to its candidate status for EU membership.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain underdeveloped, with limited domestic regulatory bodies specifically tasked with AI oversight and compliance monitoring as of March 2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (applied through harmonization)

Regulation Status: Germany implements the EU AI Act (2024) as the primary regulatory framework, applying a risk-based approach that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk to low-risk with transparency obligations. National implementation measures and sector-specific guidelines complement the EU framework, with ongoing development of liability regimes for AI systems.
Policy Lever: Germany employs data protection principles, human rights safeguards, and democratic governance mechanisms as core policy levers, supported by transparency and accountability requirements. Institutional mechanisms focus on risk mitigation through regulatory bodies and compliance oversight.
Governance Type: Germany operates a multi-level governance structure combining EU regulatory frameworks with national implementation through federal authorities, including the Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) and the Data Protection Commissioner. Model governance and organizational oversight are integrated through sector-specific regulators.
Actor Involvement: Governance involves the EU Commission and Parliament as primary rulemakers, with German national authorities (Federal Government, Bundestag, state governments) implementing and enforcing regulations. International coordination occurs through EU mechanisms and bilateral agreements on AI standards and interoperability.
Enforcement Level: Active enforcement occurs through EU and national authorities with administrative fines up to 6% of global turnover for high-risk AI violations, ongoing compliance monitoring, and sector-specific oversight bodies conducting audits and investigations.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), German AI Act Implementation Ordinance (2024-2025), Digital Services Act (2024), Digital Markets Act (2024), General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR 2018)

Regulation Status: Ghana follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union frameworks and international coordination rather than domestic legislation. The country is in early stages of developing AI policy through regional initiatives and documentation without comprehensive national AI laws enacted.
Policy Lever: Ghana's policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union initiatives. Emphasis remains on voluntary guidelines and case-by-case organizational governance rather than binding regulatory instruments.
Governance Type: Ghana employs a decentralized governance model combining case-by-case organizational governance with efforts to develop private sector engagement and human-centered approaches. Governance structures are still developing through collaboration with international bodies rather than established domestic institutions.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the African Union, G7, and UNDP providing external governance frameworks and capacity building. Ghana participates in these multilateral initiatives but lacks prominent domestic governmental AI policy leadership bodies.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with reliance on international coordination and voluntary compliance rather than domestic regulatory bodies or penalty structures.

Regulation Status: Greece implements the EU AI Act (2024) as the primary regulatory framework, applying a risk-based approach to AI systems across all sectors. National implementation measures and coordination mechanisms have been established to ensure compliance with European standards, while Greece participates in EU-wide governance structures for AI oversight.
Policy Lever: Greece employs data protection, human rights alignment, and democratic safeguards as key policy mechanisms, leveraging GDPR compliance and AI Act requirements. The country prioritizes institutional capacity-building and transparency obligations to mitigate AI-related risks to democracy and rule of law.
Governance Type: Greece operates under a dual governance structure combining EU-level regulatory frameworks with national implementation bodies and competent authorities. Organizational governance focuses on model governance compliance, data handling standards, and institutional oversight mechanisms coordinated with EU institutions.
Actor Involvement: Greece's AI regulation operates within the EU regulatory framework as a member state, involving the European Commission, national competent authorities, and intergovernmental coordination through EDPB and AI Act regulatory bodies. Multiple stakeholders including public institutions, private sector, and civil society participate in governance at both national and EU levels.
Enforcement Level: Greece enforces AI Act compliance through national competent authorities with powers to conduct audits, impose administrative fines up to €30 million or 6% of global revenue, and issue corrective orders for non-compliant high-risk systems.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act 2024, GDPR 2018, Greek Data Protection Law 4624/2019, EU AI Liability Directive (draft/implementation pending)

Regulation Status: Grenada has no dedicated national AI regulation or policy framework as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on participation in regional initiatives rather than domestic legislation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to regional declarations and data protection frameworks inherited from Commonwealth arrangements. No AI-specific policy instruments have been implemented domestically.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily through regional organizational participation rather than domestic institutional structures. Grenada lacks a dedicated national AI governance body or committee.
Actor Involvement: Grenada participates in the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and signed the Santiago Declaration (2023). Involvement remains at regional level with minimal domestic institutional actors.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-specific regulation at the national level.

Regulation Status: Guatemala has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on participation in regional frameworks and declarations rather than domestic statutory regulation. AI governance remains largely informal and unstructured at the national level.
Policy Lever: Guatemala's AI policy approach is centered on international commitments and regional cooperation mechanisms, particularly through the Santiago Declaration. Data protection frameworks exist but are not AI-specific.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational and regional in nature, with Guatemala engaging through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean (IALAC). Domestic institutional capacity for AI governance remains limited.
Actor Involvement: Guatemala participates in regional multilateral frameworks, particularly IALAC and the Santiago Declaration initiative signed in 2023. Involvement is regional and declaratory rather than involving multiple domestic actors.
Enforcement Level: No specific enforcement mechanisms exist for AI governance in Guatemala, as the country lacks dedicated legislation and relies on voluntary adherence to regional declarations.

Regulation Status: Guinea follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance largely through African Union frameworks and international partnerships rather than domestic legislation. The country has not enacted specific national AI laws but is engaged with continental initiatives promoting responsible AI development.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are being promoted as policy mechanisms, primarily through African Union guidance rather than implemented national controls. Guinea's approach emphasizes data sovereignty principles within broader continental frameworks.
Governance Type: Guinea employs case-by-case and organizational governance with emphasis on developing private sector engagement and human-centered approaches. Governance structures remain nascent, with coordination primarily through African Union institutional frameworks rather than dedicated national AI governance bodies.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary international actors shaping Guinea's AI governance approach through technical assistance and policy advocacy. Guinea participates in continental AI initiatives but maintains limited independent domestic governance infrastructure.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with oversight primarily reliant on international coordination frameworks rather than domestic regulatory bodies with enforcement capacity.

Regulation Status: Guinea-Bissau has minimal domestic AI regulation and relies primarily on continental frameworks promoted by the African Union. The country is adopting a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance through AU documentation and international partnerships rather than standalone national legislation.
Policy Lever: Primary policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls aligned with AU guidelines. Implementation remains largely aspirational with limited domestic enforcement infrastructure.
Governance Type: Guinea-Bissau follows a developing private-sector and human-centered approach to AI governance coordinated through international actors. Governance structures remain nascent with emphasis on capacity-building and alignment with continental standards.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary drivers of AI policy engagement in Guinea-Bissau, providing technical support and strategic direction. Regional frameworks supersede domestic governance structures given limited national institutional capacity.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal due to limited institutional capacity and absence of dedicated national AI regulatory bodies or statutory enforcement agencies.

Regulation Status: Guyana has minimal dedicated AI regulation, relying primarily on participation in regional frameworks rather than domestic legislation. The country has signed onto the Santiago Declaration but lacks comprehensive national AI laws or a formal regulatory framework.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data governance and regional cooperation through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean. Guyana relies on voluntary alignment with regional guidelines rather than binding domestic policy instruments.
Governance Type: Governance operates primarily at the regional/organizational level through the CAIAIC framework, with limited domestic institutional structures dedicated to AI oversight. No national AI agency or dedicated regulatory body has been established.
Actor Involvement: Guyana participates in the regional Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and has endorsed the Santiago Declaration (2023). Involvement remains primarily through regional associations rather than multilateral or bilateral agreements.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are absent; reliance on voluntary compliance with regional declarations rather than legal enforcement structures.

Regulation Status: Haiti has no dedicated national AI regulation or policy framework as of March 2026. The country lacks specific legislation governing artificial intelligence development, deployment, or use.
Policy Lever: Haiti's engagement with AI governance is primarily through regional declarations rather than domestic policy mechanisms. The country is party to the Santiago Declaration (2023) through its membership in regional organizations, representing a voluntary, non-binding commitment.
Governance Type: Haiti lacks a dedicated national governance structure for AI regulation. Governance remains fragmented with no designated ministry, agency, or coordinating body responsible for AI oversight or policy development.
Actor Involvement: Haiti participates in regional AI governance through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and has signed the Santiago Declaration. Domestic stakeholder engagement in AI governance remains minimal.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI governance in Haiti, as the country lacks comprehensive national legislation or regulatory bodies with enforcement authority.

Regulation Status: Honduras has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on its participation in regional frameworks, particularly the Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI signed in 2023 through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean. Governance remains largely organizational rather than legislative.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are limited to regional declarations and data-focused approaches through international cooperation. No comprehensive national AI policy framework or binding regulatory instruments have been formally established.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational and regional, coordinated through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean rather than through dedicated national institutions. Honduras lacks a dedicated national AI governance body or regulatory agency.
Actor Involvement: Honduras engages through regional multilateral mechanisms, particularly the Latin American and Caribbean regional council on AI. Involvement remains limited to international agreements without substantial domestic institutional actors in AI governance.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist; compliance with regional declarations is voluntary and lacks binding enforcement capacity within Honduras.

Regulation Status: Hungary operates under the EU AI Act framework, which entered into force in 2024 and is being implemented progressively through 2026. The country applies the risk-based regulatory approach across all sectors, categorizing AI systems as prohibited, high-risk, or general-risk with corresponding obligations.
Policy Lever: Hungary leverages data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic integrity as policy mechanisms through the EU AI Act implementation and national compliance frameworks. The approach emphasizes mitigating systemic risks to democracy and rule of law through institutional oversight and transparency requirements.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily model-based and organizational, with designated notified bodies and competent authorities responsible for oversight of high-risk AI systems. Hungary operates within the EU's centralized governance structure while maintaining national authorities for enforcement and coordination.
Actor Involvement: Hungary's AI regulation involves multiple actors including the EU Commission, national data protection authorities, sectoral regulators, and market surveillance authorities operating at both EU and national levels. International coordination occurs through EU institutions, with Member States sharing enforcement responsibilities under the AI Act framework.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through national competent authorities with powers to conduct audits, issue compliance orders, and apply administrative fines up to €30 million or 6% of global turnover for high-risk AI violations, with escalating compliance deadlines through 2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), EU AI Liability Directive (draft/pending), Hungarian Data Protection Act alignment (GDPR implementation)

Regulation Status: Iceland applies the EU AI Act as an EEA member state, implementing a risk-based regulatory framework that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to low-risk with corresponding compliance obligations. The country operates within the broader EU regulatory architecture while developing complementary national oversight mechanisms.
Policy Lever: Iceland leverages data protection laws, human rights frameworks, and democratic governance principles to guide AI regulation, with emphasis on algorithmic transparency and public accountability. The policy approach integrates EU standards with domestic considerations for democratic institutions and rule of law.
Governance Type: Iceland employs a hybrid governance model combining EU-level regulatory requirements with national implementation through domestic authorities and oversight bodies. This includes model-specific governance for high-risk systems and organizational accountability mechanisms for AI developers and deployers.
Actor Involvement: Iceland's AI regulation operates at multiple levels: EU institutions setting framework rules, Icelandic national authorities (including data protection office and relevant sectoral regulators) implementing and enforcing standards, and participation in EEA/EFTA governance structures. International coordination occurs through EU mechanisms and bilateral engagement with other Nordic and European countries.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through Iceland's data protection authority and sectoral regulators applying EU AI Act provisions, with penalties and compliance mechanisms in place, though enforcement capacity varies by sector.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Icelandic Data Protection Act (aligned with GDPR), EU AI Liability Directive (draft/emerging)

Regulation Status: India is in the active drafting phase for AI-specific regulation, with amendments to the IT Act underway and the Digital India Act in development. The country balances innovation promotion with emerging regulatory frameworks, while participating in BRICS AI coordination initiatives.
Policy Lever: India employs data governance and computing infrastructure as primary policy mechanisms, leveraging the Digital Personal Data Protection Act framework and data localization requirements. Computing resource allocation and data flow controls serve as key regulatory tools.
Governance Type: India utilizes a hybrid governance model combining case-by-case review with modernized digital governance structures under the DPDP Act framework. Multiple ministries coordinate on AI policy with evolving sectoral oversight mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: Governance involves central government agencies, regional technology hubs, and BRICS member coordination on AI standards. Scope remains primarily intra-Asian with emerging multilateral engagement through BRICS AI-specific regulation initiatives.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains nascent with reliance on existing data protection authorities and sectoral regulators, pending completion of comprehensive AI legislation with dedicated enforcement mechanisms.
Specific Laws: Information Technology Act (amendments in progress), Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023, Digital India Act (forthcoming)

Regulation Status: Indonesia is actively developing AI-specific regulation with draft AI ethics guidelines released and comprehensive AI regulation framework in progress. The country is balancing innovation with governance through sector-specific approaches, particularly in healthcare and critical infrastructure, while aligning with ASEAN AI principles.
Policy Lever: Indonesia leverages data governance, computing infrastructure requirements, and sector-specific standards as primary policy mechanisms. The government emphasizes Indonesia's role as a regional data hub while implementing data localization and computing capacity controls.
Governance Type: Indonesia employs a hybrid governance model combining organizational oversight through established ministries with case-by-case governance for high-risk AI applications. Decision-making involves multiple agencies coordinating on healthcare AI, digital infrastructure, and national digital transformation initiatives.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include Ministry of Communication and Digital Infrastructure, Ministry of Health, industry stakeholders, and academic institutions, operating within ASEAN AI governance frameworks while increasingly aligning with global standards. Indonesia actively participates in regional AI policy coordination through ASEAN and engages with international AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain limited and primarily advisory, with soft governance through guidelines and industry coordination rather than statutory penalties, though regulatory infrastructure for enforcement is being established.

Regulation Status: Iran maintains a risk-based regulatory approach focused on leveraging AI for state objectives rather than comprehensive citizen protection frameworks. The approach prioritizes national security and governmental control over independent oversight mechanisms.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms center on data-driven administrative support for R&D initiatives and digital economy development, with emphasis on intelligent systems deployment for law enforcement. Government maintains control over AI application through national strategy alignment rather than independent regulatory bodies.
Governance Type: Governance is centralized and case-by-case organizational, with Iranian authorities directing deployment of AI systems including facial recognition and surveillance technology for law enforcement purposes. This reflects a state-led approach integrated with governmental security objectives rather than independent regulatory frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Primarily national-level actors within Iranian government and state institutions drive AI policy, with explicit non-adoption of regional regulatory standards. Iran pursues independent AI development capacity with stated goal of becoming an AI power by 2032.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement operates through governmental directives and case-by-case oversight, with deployment of surveillance and facial recognition systems indicating some active implementation, though transparency and independent audit mechanisms remain limited.

Regulation Status: Iraq has no comprehensive AI regulation framework as of March 2026, but maintains a national AI strategy focused on intelligence applications (2023). Recent ministerial calls for social media AI regulation indicate emerging policy interest, though implementation remains nascent.
Policy Lever: Iraq employs data-driven mechanisms including export controls and R&D spending to influence AI development. These levers are underdeveloped and lack integrated coordination across sectors.
Governance Type: Governance remains ad-hoc and case-by-case, with fragmented approaches to data privacy, security, and transparency. Iraq aspires toward international regulatory alignment for social media content and AI, but institutional frameworks are still emerging.
Actor Involvement: Iraq positions itself as a regional telecommunications hub with aspirations for international AI significance comparable to strategic infrastructure. Involvement includes government ministries, intelligence agencies, and international telecommunications entities, with regional and potentially global scope.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal; no established penalties, audits, or dedicated oversight bodies for AI regulation exist.

Regulation Status: Ireland operates under the EU AI Act (effective since August 2024), which applies a risk-based regulatory framework across all sectors. The country also engages with the proposed EU AI Liability Directive and transposed relevant EU digital governance requirements into national law.
Policy Lever: Ireland emphasizes data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic accountability through GDPR compliance and AI Act implementation. The policy framework prioritizes transparency obligations and risk mitigation through institutional oversight and stakeholder engagement.
Governance Type: Governance is structured through both organizational oversight (via Data Protection Commission and sectoral regulators) and algorithmic/model governance mechanisms mandated by the EU AI Act. Ireland coordinates national implementation with EU-level regulatory bodies and maintains institutional capacity for compliance monitoring.
Actor Involvement: Ireland's AI regulation operates primarily within the EU regulatory framework, with the European Commission, national Data Protection Authority, and sectoral regulators as key actors. International coordination occurs through EU institutions and intergovernmental agreements, with private sector engagement through compliance requirements.
Enforcement Level: The Irish Data Protection Commission enforces AI regulations through compliance audits, investigations, and administrative fines under EU AI Act provisions, with escalating penalties for non-compliance and prohibited AI systems.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR, 2018), Digital Markets Act (2022), ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC)

Regulation Status: Israel maintains a sectoral, risk-based approach to AI regulation as outlined in its 2023 Policy on AI: Regulation and Ethics, with no comprehensive AI-specific legislation yet enacted. Draft legislation including the Bots Law and regulatory frameworks continue to be developed incrementally rather than through a single omnibus law.
Policy Lever: Israel employs soft regulatory tools, sectoral regulation, and cooperative mechanisms between public and private actors to develop the regulatory framework incrementally. The approach emphasizes stakeholder engagement and phased implementation rather than prescriptive mandates.
Governance Type: Governance is structured around organizational and model-based frameworks with coordination between governmental bodies and private sector actors. Multiple ministries and agencies oversee different sectors under the sectoral regulation approach.
Actor Involvement: Both governmental and private sector actors are involved at the national level, with potential for regional engagement particularly in technology hubs and international cooperation frameworks. Industry participation in policy development remains significant.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are limited given the lack of comprehensive legislation, relying primarily on soft enforcement through guidance documents, industry cooperation, and sectoral oversight rather than active penalties or audits.
Specific Laws: Israel Policy on AI: Regulation and Ethics (2023), Draft Bots Law (2022), Draft Regulation and Ethics Policy In the Field of Artificial Intelligence (2022)

Regulation Status: Italy operates under the EU AI Act (effective since 2024), implementing a risk-based regulatory framework across all sectors with prohibited, high-risk, and lower-risk AI system categories. The AI Liability Directive is under finalization at EU level, which will establish harmonized liability rules applicable to Italian entities.
Policy Lever: Italy employs data protection, human rights safeguards, and rule of law principles as core regulatory mechanisms through GDPR and AI Act compliance. The government emphasizes institutional capacity-building and democratic governance protections in AI deployment.
Governance Type: Italy combines organizational governance through national AI authorities and implementation bodies with model governance requirements mandated by the EU AI Act. The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) and newly established AI governance structures oversee compliance.
Actor Involvement: Regulation operates at multiple levels: EU framework (AI Act, Liability Directive) with national implementation by Italian government and regulators, alongside intergovernmental coordination through EU institutions. International alignment with OECD principles and G7/G20 AI governance initiatives.
Enforcement Level: The Garante and designated national competent authorities conduct audits, investigations, and enforce AI Act provisions with fines up to 6% of global revenue, though full enforcement capacity is still ramping up as of early 2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), EU AI Liability Directive (pending finalization), GDPR (2018, as amended)

Regulation Status: Jamaica has not enacted dedicated AI legislation as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on the Santiago Declaration framework signed in 2023 through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean, which promotes ethical AI principles without binding regulatory requirements.
Policy Lever: Jamaica's approach centers on data governance and ethical principles through regional declarations rather than domestic statutory mechanisms. No formal national AI strategy or dedicated policy instruments have been established.
Governance Type: Governance operates at the organizational and regional level through the Intergovernmental Council on AI for Latin America and the Caribbean, with limited domestic institutional structures dedicated to AI oversight. Jamaica has not established a dedicated national AI governance body.
Actor Involvement: Jamaica participates in regional AI governance through the Latin American and Caribbean Intergovernmental Council, signaling commitment to regional AI frameworks. Domestic actor involvement remains limited with no evidence of significant private sector or civil society coordinated AI policy initiatives.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation in Jamaica; compliance with regional declarations is voluntary and non-binding.

Regulation Status: Japan maintains a risk-based approach to AI regulation with established ethical principles and multiple guidelines, but lacks comprehensive binding legislation as of early 2026. The country continues developing AI-specific regulations while actively shaping international standards through the Hiroshima AI Process and G7 leadership.
Policy Lever: Japan employs data protection frameworks, algorithm governance mechanisms, and strategic chip export controls as primary policy levers. These tools support both domestic AI development and international competitiveness objectives.
Governance Type: Governance operates through a combination of organizational guidelines, industry-led principles, and case-by-case business and social principle frameworks. Multiple stakeholders including government, industry, and regional actors participate in setting AI standards.
Actor Involvement: National government, regional authorities, and private AI technology companies are deeply involved in advancing AI development for economic growth. International coordination through G7 and OECD mechanisms reflects Japan's commitment to shaping global AI governance.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains limited to voluntary compliance with principles and guidelines; formal legal enforcement mechanisms are underdeveloped as binding legislation has not yet been fully enacted.
Specific Laws: Social Principles for Human-Centric AI (principles framework)

Regulation Status: Jordan is implementing a risk-based regulatory approach through its National AI Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023-2027, which guides AI governance without yet establishing comprehensive binding legislation. The country is in the strategy development and ethical framework phase rather than having enacted specific AI laws.
Policy Lever: Jordan is leveraging a National AI Code of Ethics as its primary policy mechanism, supported by data-driven initiatives to establish ethical standards for AI deployment. The approach emphasizes ethical guidelines and compliance frameworks rather than prescriptive legal mandates.
Governance Type: Jordan's governance structure is case and organizationally focused, with emphasis on building compliance monitoring systems and reviewing the legislative environment to align with AI ethics standards. The system prioritizes updating regulatory frameworks to support national AI objectives.
Actor Involvement: The National AI Strategy involves government coordination at the national level with focus on institutional capacity building and multi-stakeholder engagement across public sector organizations. Implementation is primarily domestically focused with limited international coordination mentioned in available documentation.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain in early stages with focus on developing compliance systems rather than active penalty-based enforcement; oversight bodies are being established as part of the strategic implementation.
Specific Laws: National AI Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023-2027, National AI Code of Ethics (in development)

Regulation Status: Kazakhstan has no comprehensive AI regulation enacted as of March 2026, but the country is advancing through draft legislation and strategic frameworks. The CIS-approved draft model law on AI regulation and ethical standards represents a regional approach, while Kazakhstan continues developing its national AI strategy aligned with governmental digitalization goals.
Policy Lever: Kazakhstan employs data governance and computing infrastructure as primary policy levers through initiatives like Smart Data Ukimet, which centralizes government data collection and processing. The focus remains on data standardization and digital infrastructure development rather than direct AI model regulation.
Governance Type: Kazakhstan is developing hybrid governance combining model-level oversight (through draft CIS law) with organizational and infrastructure governance. Government agencies lead implementation through the National AI Development Concept, emphasizing state coordination of AI deployment across public sector.
Actor Involvement: Multiple governmental actors are actively involved, including ministries coordinating through Smart Data Ukimet and the National AI Development Concept. Regional engagement occurs through CIS cooperation, though implementation remains primarily national through government agencies and state infrastructure projects.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain minimal as binding regulation has not been enacted; oversight exists primarily through governmental coordination of data and infrastructure initiatives rather than through legal penalties or dedicated enforcement bodies.
Specific Laws: CIS Draft Model Law on AI Regulation and Ethical Standards (year unspecified)

Regulation Status: Kenya has introduced draft legislation on AI and robotics and developed national strategies aligned with the African Union Continental Strategy on AI, but comprehensive binding regulation has not yet been enacted as of March 2026. The country is pursuing an innovative, human-centered approach to AI governance with emphasis on leveraging AI for sustainable development.
Policy Lever: Kenya employs data governance and computing export controls as primary policy mechanisms, combined with digital ID frameworks and sector-specific oversight. The approach emphasizes capacity building and private sector engagement rather than restrictive mandates.
Governance Type: Kenya utilizes a hybrid governance model combining case-by-case organizational oversight with private sector development and multi-stakeholder consultation. This reflects a human-centered approach that engages both government and industry in shaping AI policy.
Actor Involvement: Kenya participates actively in regional governance through EARFAI and has adopted the Nairobi Statement on AI and Emerging Technologies, demonstrating commitment to East African and continental coordination. Domestic actors include the ICT Ministry, private sector stakeholders, and civil society organizations engaged in AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain limited as primary legislation is still in draft stage, with oversight primarily through voluntary guidelines and informal coordination rather than formal penalties or systematic audits.
Specific Laws: Draft Bill on AI and Robotics

Regulation Status: Kiribati has no dedicated AI regulation or policy framework as of March 2026. The country lacks specific legislation governing artificial intelligence development, deployment, or use.
Policy Lever: No formal policy mechanisms or levers for AI governance are in place. Kiribati relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks if applicable.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structure or institutional body exists. AI-related matters would fall under general government portfolios responsible for technology or communications.
Actor Involvement: Limited actor involvement; governance would involve the Ministry of Information, Communication and Transport if engaged. International engagement is primarily through participation in Pacific regional organizations rather than bilateral AI policy frameworks.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms for AI governance exist due to the absence of specific AI regulation.

Regulation Status: Kosovo applies the EU AI Act framework through its EU integration pathway and candidate status, with a risk-based approach to AI regulation. The country is developing its own AI governance structures while aligning with EU standards, though comprehensive national AI legislation remains in draft form.
Policy Lever: Kosovo emphasizes data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic governance in its AI policy approach. The country leverages international agreements and EU frameworks to mitigate AI risks through institutional capacity-building.
Governance Type: Kosovo employs organizational and model-based governance with oversight structures emerging at national level. Governance is structured through EU compliance mechanisms and domestic institutional development, with multiple stakeholders involved.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the Kosovo government, EU institutions through approximation requirements, and regional intergovernmental bodies. Kosovo participates in EU-level AI governance as a candidate country, with increasing involvement of national regulators and civil society.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains limited, with capacity-building ongoing and reliance on EU oversight mechanisms; domestic enforcement structures are developing but not yet fully operational.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024, applicable), Draft AI Liability Directive (EU-level)

Regulation Status: Kuwait has not enacted comprehensive AI-specific legislation as of March 2026. The country relies primarily on existing telecommunications and data protection frameworks, with emerging awareness of AI governance needs reflected in national digital transformation initiatives.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms are largely voluntary and sector-specific, focusing on guidelines from telecommunications regulators and alignment with GCC-level initiatives. Kuwait has engaged with international AI governance discussions but lacks binding domestic policy instruments.
Governance Type: Governance is fragmented across multiple ministries (Communications, Interior, Commerce) without a dedicated central AI authority. Decision-making remains decentralized with limited institutional coordination on AI policy.
Actor Involvement: Government agencies and telecom regulators are primary actors, with limited private sector and academic institution involvement in formal governance structures. International engagement occurs primarily through GCC cooperation frameworks rather than bilateral agreements.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated AI enforcement bodies or structured oversight infrastructure specific to artificial intelligence applications.

Regulation Status: Kyrgyzstan has a Digital Code (2023) that provides a comprehensive legal framework addressing digital technologies including AI, with regulations on personal data protection and technology deployment. Implementation of AI-specific provisions remains in early stages with ongoing development of sectoral guidance.
Policy Lever: Primary policy mechanisms focus on data protection and technology deployment oversight through the Digital Code framework. Alignment with CIS (Commonwealth of Independent States) recommendations guides regulatory approach.
Governance Type: Governance emphasizes a model-based approach centered on legal frameworks for data protection and technology regulation, with coordination with CIS standards. Institutional mechanisms for AI-specific oversight are still developing.
Actor Involvement: Regulation is primarily national-level, driven by government through the Digital Code framework. Multi-stakeholder engagement in AI policy development remains limited.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms exist for data protection under the Digital Code but specific AI enforcement capacity and active monitoring remain underdeveloped.
Specific Laws: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic (2023)

Regulation Status: Laos maintains an innovation-based approach aligned with ASEAN frameworks, relying primarily on ethics and governance guides rather than binding legislation. No comprehensive national AI regulation has been enacted as of March 2026, though the country continues to develop policy positions within the regional ASEAN context.
Policy Lever: Laos employs data-driven policy mechanisms and participates in ASEAN-level initiatives, but lacks specific domestic policy instruments for AI governance. The approach remains largely advisory and non-binding at the national level.
Governance Type: Laos utilizes case-by-case and model-based governance approaches, deferring to ASEAN coordination rather than establishing independent regulatory bodies. Governance remains decentralized and informal without dedicated AI oversight agencies.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include ASEAN member coordination bodies and limited domestic stakeholder engagement, with governance primarily operating through regional frameworks. Limited evidence of multi-stakeholder involvement or civil society participation in AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated agencies or penalty structures for AI-related violations at the national level.

Regulation Status: Latvia applies the EU AI Act as a binding regional regulation, implementing a risk-based approach that categorizes AI systems into prohibited, high-risk, and other categories with varying compliance obligations. The country transposed EU requirements into national frameworks and participates in EU-wide enforcement mechanisms. A draft AI liability directive is under development at the EU level, which will further extend regulatory coverage.
Policy Lever: Latvia leverages data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic accountability mechanisms as primary policy tools to mitigate AI risks. The approach emphasizes institutional strengthening and transparency obligations to protect rule of law and fundamental rights.
Governance Type: Governance operates through a multi-layered system combining organizational oversight of AI developers and deployers with model governance requirements. Latvia integrates EU institutional frameworks with national competent authorities responsible for enforcement and compliance monitoring.
Actor Involvement: Regulation is primarily driven by EU-level frameworks (European Commission, national regulators) with Latvia as an EU member state implementing and enforcing requirements regionally. Involvement spans intergovernmental institutions, national data protection authorities, and sectoral regulators across all member states.
Enforcement Level: EU AI Act enforcement is conducted through national competent authorities and market surveillance mechanisms with significant penalties (up to 6% of global turnover for violations), though full enforcement maturity is still developing as of March 2026.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2023), Data Protection Regulation (GDPR 2018)

Regulation Status: Lebanon has no AI-specific legislation enacted as of 2026. The 2019 National AI Strategy in Lebanese Industry provides a non-binding framework for AI development, and cybersecurity regulations exist but do not directly address AI governance.
Policy Lever: Lebanon relies primarily on cybersecurity frameworks and general data protection measures rather than AI-specific policy mechanisms. There is limited algorithmic accountability infrastructure or sector-specific AI guidelines.
Governance Type: Governance remains fragmented with limited formal AI coordination at the national level. The approach is primarily reactive and case-by-case rather than proactive regulatory design.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is largely restricted to government agencies and select industry stakeholders, with minimal participation from civil society or international bodies. Regional economic instability has limited resources for comprehensive AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated enforcement mechanisms for AI governance exist; enforcement relies on existing cybersecurity and data protection authorities with limited capacity.

Regulation Status: Lesotho has no dedicated AI regulation or comprehensive AI policy framework as of March 2026. The country has not enacted specific legislation governing artificial intelligence systems or their deployment.
Policy Lever: No specific policy mechanisms targeting AI have been implemented. Lesotho relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks that predate AI-specific considerations.
Governance Type: No designated AI governance body or coordinating agency exists in Lesotho. AI-related matters, if addressed at all, fall under general technology and telecommunications oversight.
Actor Involvement: Involvement is limited to international participation through SADC (Southern African Development Community) and African Union frameworks. Domestic private sector and academic engagement in AI policy discussions is minimal.
Enforcement Level: No AI-specific enforcement mechanisms, compliance frameworks, or oversight bodies are operational in Lesotho.

Regulation Status: Liberia is in the early stages of AI governance, primarily through participation in African Union human-rights-centered frameworks and regional initiatives rather than domestic legislation. The country lacks comprehensive national AI legislation but engages with continental blueprints and resolutions promoting responsible AI development.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union coordination, though implementation at the national level remains nascent. Liberia relies on international agreements and frameworks rather than independent policy instruments.
Governance Type: Governance follows a case-by-case and organizational approach centered on private sector engagement and human-rights principles aligned with African Union directives. The structure emphasizes multi-stakeholder participation rather than formal institutional regulatory bodies.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the African Union (continental level), G7 engagement on global standards, and UNDP support for capacity building and policy development. Liberia participates in these initiatives as a member state but exercises limited independent policy leverage.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal with no dedicated national AI regulatory body or active penalty structures; oversight occurs primarily through voluntary compliance with international frameworks.

Regulation Status: Libya follows a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance promoted through African Union frameworks and documentation, but lacks domestic legislation specifically addressing AI regulation. The country relies on regional guidance through AU initiatives rather than national AI-specific laws.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are identified as a policy mechanism, though implementation appears limited. Libya's participation in broader African frameworks suggests theoretical alignment with data governance principles.
Governance Type: Libya is developing a case-by-case and organizational governance approach emphasizing human-centered AI development and private sector engagement. Governance structures remain nascent with limited institutional capacity for systematic AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are involved in promoting AI governance frameworks applicable to Libya, with AU playing the primary regional role. International actor involvement exceeds domestic institutional capacity.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with reliance on voluntary compliance through international guidelines rather than binding national enforcement structures.

Regulation Status: Liechtenstein applies the EU AI Act through its participation in the EEA framework, implementing a risk-based regulatory approach that categorizes AI systems as prohibited, high-risk, or subject to transparency obligations. The country has adopted EU-level AI governance without developing separate national AI legislation.
Policy Lever: Liechtenstein leverages the EU's data protection and human rights frameworks (GDPR, Charter of Fundamental Rights) as policy mechanisms for AI governance. The country emphasizes institutional safeguards and democratic accountability through adoption of EU-level standards.
Governance Type: Liechtenstein operates within a supranational EU/EEA governance structure with organizational oversight delegated to national authorities and EU bodies. Model governance is coordinated through European supervisory authorities and national data protection offices.
Actor Involvement: Governance involves multiple actors including EU institutions (European Commission, European Board on AI), national regulatory bodies (Office of Justice/Data Protection Commissioner), and intergovernmental coordination through EEA mechanisms. Liechtenstein participates at regional and international levels through EU/EEA frameworks.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through EU-level mechanisms and national authorities with powers to conduct audits, impose fines, and issue corrective orders under the AI Act framework, though Liechtenstein's small size relies heavily on coordinated EU enforcement infrastructure.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), GDPR (2018), EEA Agreement provisions on AI governance

Regulation Status: Lithuania implements the EU AI Act as part of the European Union's harmonized risk-based regulatory framework, which became applicable in 2024-2025. The country operates within this supranational legal structure that categorizes AI systems as prohibited, high-risk, or low-risk with corresponding compliance obligations.
Policy Lever: Lithuania leverages data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic governance principles through the EU AI Act implementation and national adaptation measures. The approach emphasizes institutional oversight and mitigating long-term risks through established regulatory bodies and transparency requirements.
Governance Type: Lithuania employs organizational and model-level governance through designated competent authorities responsible for monitoring high-risk AI systems and enforcing compliance. Governance is coordinated at both national and EU levels, with the National Data Protection Authority and sector-specific regulators sharing oversight responsibilities.
Actor Involvement: Regulation is driven by the EU-level AI Act framework with active participation from Lithuania's government institutions, national regulators, and coordination with European Commission bodies. International agreements and EU harmonization create multilayered governance involving supranational, national, and sectoral actors.
Enforcement Level: The EU AI Act enforcement mechanism includes significant penalties for non-compliance (up to 6% of global turnover for prohibited systems, 3% for high-risk violations), with national authorities conducting inspections and audits, though full enforcement maturity continues to develop as the regulatory framework is operationalized.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2023, applicable 2024-2025), GDPR (2018), ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC as amended)

Regulation Status: Luxembourg implements the EU AI Act as the primary binding regulation, with a risk-based framework classifying AI systems from prohibited to low-risk categories. The country applies EU-wide standards through national transposition and enforcement mechanisms aligned with the broader European regulatory architecture.
Policy Lever: Luxembourg leverages data protection, human rights, and democratic safeguards as policy levers through the EU AI Act framework. The country emphasizes institutional capacity-building and compliance oversight to mitigate AI-related risks to rule of law and individual rights.
Governance Type: Governance operates through a hybrid model combining organizational oversight (national competent authorities) with algorithmic and model governance standards set by EU regulations. Luxembourg coordinates with EU bodies including the AI Office and National Data Protection Authorities for implementation and enforcement.
Actor Involvement: Regulation operates primarily at the EU regional level through Luxembourg's participation in European Union governance structures, with involvement from national authorities, the European Commission, and intergovernmental cooperation mechanisms. The country implements EU-mandated requirements while maintaining national competencies for specific enforcement.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through Luxembourg's national authorities (primarily the National Commission for Data Protection and regulatory bodies) working with EU coordination mechanisms, including compliance audits, administrative fines, and market surveillance for high-risk AI systems.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), EU AI Liability Directive (draft/adopted phase as of 2026)

Regulation Status: Macedonia applies the EU AI Act as an EU member state, implementing a risk-based approach that categorizes AI systems from prohibited to high-risk with corresponding transparency obligations. The country has transposed EU AI Act requirements into its national legal framework and is working on implementing the AI Liability Directive.
Policy Lever: Macedonia uses data protection, human rights, and rule of law principles as core policy levers, leveraging institutional frameworks to mitigate AI risks. The approach emphasizes democratic safeguards and institutional oversight aligned with EU values.
Governance Type: Macedonia employs organizational and model governance structures, with designated authorities responsible for AI oversight and compliance monitoring. Governance integrates both sectoral and cross-cutting institutional mechanisms.
Actor Involvement: EU-level governance dominates through the EU AI Act framework, with Macedonia as a member state responsible for national implementation. Key actors include EU institutions, national regulators, and sectoral bodies operating within the EU regulatory ecosystem.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through a combination of national competent authorities and EU oversight mechanisms, with developing capacity for monitoring compliance and issuing penalties aligned with EU standards.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), AI Liability Directive (draft), Macedonia AI Implementation Framework (transposition status)

Regulation Status: Madagascar has not yet enacted dedicated AI legislation but is engaged with continental AI governance frameworks promoted by the African Union. The country is following a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance aligned with AU initiatives, though domestic implementation remains nascent.
Policy Lever: Madagascar operates primarily through data and computing export controls as part of broader African Union coordination rather than dedicated AI policy mechanisms. No specific Madagascar-level AI policy levers have been formally implemented as of early 2026.
Governance Type: Madagascar's AI governance is emerging through case-by-case and organizational approaches with emphasis on private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Governance structures remain underdeveloped with reliance on international frameworks rather than domestic institutional capacity.
Actor Involvement: Madagascar is engaged through African Union initiatives, with support from international actors including G7 coordination and UNDP technical assistance. Involvement is primarily at the continental and UN system levels rather than through bilateral arrangements.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated AI enforcement mechanisms exist at the national level; oversight is primarily through international coordination frameworks without binding domestic enforcement capacity.

Regulation Status: Malawi participates in the African Union's human rights-centered approach to AI governance through regional frameworks and documentation, but lacks domestically enacted AI-specific legislation. The country is in an early stage of AI regulation, primarily influenced by continental initiatives rather than national policy.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are identified as a policy mechanism, though implementation details remain limited. Malawi's approach is largely aspirational, aligned with AU recommendations rather than actively deployed regulatory tools.
Governance Type: Governance follows a case-by-case and organizational approach focused on developing private sector engagement and human-centered principles. Multi-stakeholder coordination is emerging but institutional capacity for AI governance remains nascent.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the African Union (regional framework), UNDP (capacity building), and G7 (international coordination), with Malawi as a participating nation. Engagement is primarily at the continental and international level rather than through domestic institutional leadership.
Enforcement Level: No active domestic enforcement mechanisms for AI regulation have been established; oversight is conceptual rather than operational.

Regulation Status: Malaysia is developing AI governance through a risk-based approach with an AI ethics framework in progress, alongside the Cyber Security Bill 2024. No dedicated AI-specific legislation has been enacted yet, but the regulatory landscape is transitioning from voluntary guidelines to structured frameworks.
Policy Lever: Malaysia uses data-driven policy mechanisms to attract FDI and develop digital economy capabilities, with targeted incentives for technology adoption and ASEAN-aligned standards. Policy focuses on positioning Malaysia as a regional AI hub while building domestic digital infrastructure.
Governance Type: Malaysia employs case and organizational governance with emphasis on developing national AI companies and sector-specific oversight. Governance structures are emerging through government agencies and industry collaboration rather than centralized regulatory bodies.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include ASEAN member coordination, Malaysian government agencies, technology companies, and FDI investors across ASEAN and broader developing economies. International alignment with ASEAN frameworks shapes Malaysia's multi-stakeholder governance approach.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain limited with reliance on voluntary compliance and industry self-regulation pending formal legislation implementation.
Specific Laws: Cyber Security Bill 2024

Regulation Status: Maldives has no dedicated AI regulation framework as of March 2026. The country lacks specific legislation governing artificial intelligence development, deployment, or use.
Policy Lever: No formal AI policy levers or mechanisms are in place. Maldives relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks without AI-specific policy instruments.
Governance Type: Governance of AI-related matters falls under the Ministry of Arts, Culture and Heritage and the Maldives Monetary Authority for financial sector applications, but no dedicated AI governance body exists.
Actor Involvement: Limited involvement from government agencies and civil society in AI governance. International engagement is minimal, with no notable participation in global AI governance initiatives or agreements.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms specific to AI regulation exist in Maldives as of 2026.

Regulation Status: Mali lacks comprehensive domestic AI regulation and relies on regional African Union frameworks promoting human rights-centered AI governance. The country is in early stages of engagement with international AI policy initiatives through AU coordination.
Policy Lever: Mali has not implemented specific data or computing export controls domestically, though it participates in African Union discussions on data governance. Policy mechanisms remain largely aspirational rather than operationalized.
Governance Type: Mali's AI governance approach is developmental, focused on capacity building and private sector engagement through international partnerships. Governance structures remain nascent with limited institutional frameworks for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, UNDP, and G7 influence Mali's AI policy direction through regional initiatives and technical assistance programs. Mali participates in continental AI governance discussions but maintains limited independent regulatory institutions.
Enforcement Level: Mali has minimal enforcement mechanisms for AI governance, with no dedicated regulatory bodies or compliance monitoring systems in place.

Regulation Status: Malta implements the EU AI Act as a member state, establishing a risk-based regulatory framework that classifies AI systems from prohibited to high-risk and other AI systems with transparency obligations. National implementation measures complement the EU framework, with ongoing legislative developments including the draft AI Liability Directive.
Policy Lever: Malta employs data protection, human rights safeguards, and democratic governance mechanisms as policy levers, focusing on mitigating future AI risks through institutional frameworks. The approach emphasizes transparency, accountability, and compliance with EU standards on fundamental rights and rule of law.
Governance Type: Malta operates under a dual governance structure combining organizational governance (national AI oversight bodies and sectoral regulators) with EU-level model governance through the European AI Board and coordinated enforcement mechanisms. Governance balances national implementation with EU harmonization requirements.
Actor Involvement: Multiple actors engage at regional and intergovernmental levels, including the Maltese government, EU institutions, national regulatory authorities, and sectoral supervisors operating within the EU AI Act framework. Malta participates in EU-wide coordination mechanisms and international AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement occurs through national competent authorities and EU bodies with powers to impose fines, conduct audits, and issue compliance orders under the AI Act, with coordination mechanisms for cross-border enforcement across EU member states.
Specific Laws: EU AI Act (2024), Draft AI Liability Directive (in progress)

Regulation Status: The Marshall Islands has not developed dedicated AI regulation or policy as of March 2026. The country lacks formal legislation, national AI strategies, or regulatory frameworks governing artificial intelligence development and deployment.
Policy Lever: No identifiable policy mechanisms or instruments for AI governance are currently in place. The Marshall Islands has not adopted voluntary guidelines, standards, or regulatory approaches.
Governance Type: No established governance structure for AI regulation exists. There is no designated regulatory body, ministry, or agency responsible for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Limited institutional capacity and minimal involvement from government, private sector, or international organizations in AI governance. The Marshall Islands' small economy and limited technological sector have not triggered AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms, compliance monitoring, or regulatory oversight for AI systems exist.

Regulation Status: Mauritania lacks dedicated national AI legislation as of March 2026. The country is aligned with African Union human-rights-centered AI governance frameworks but has not enacted binding domestic AI regulations. Policy development remains at early stages through regional coordination rather than national statute.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls exist as general trade mechanisms but lack AI-specific targeting. Mauritania has not implemented dedicated AI-focused data governance or computing resource restrictions.
Governance Type: Governance remains largely organizational and case-by-case rather than systemic. Private sector engagement in AI governance is emerging through regional African Union initiatives but lacks formal institutional structures within Mauritania.
Actor Involvement: Mauritania participates in African Union AI governance frameworks alongside G7 and UNDP engagement. Involvement is primarily through continental coordination mechanisms rather than direct bilateral regulation.
Enforcement Level: No dedicated AI enforcement mechanisms exist; oversight relies on general regulatory bodies without AI-specific compliance audits or penalties.

Regulation Status: Mauritius is adopting a human-rights-centered approach to AI governance primarily through African Union initiatives and regional frameworks rather than standalone national legislation. The country remains in early-stage engagement with AI regulation, relying on guidelines and blueprints promoted through continental bodies.
Policy Lever: Data and computing export controls are identified as policy mechanisms, though implementation appears limited. Mauritius is leveraging international frameworks rather than deploying comprehensive domestic policy levers.
Governance Type: Mauritius employs case-by-case and organizational governance with emphasis on developing private sector engagement and human-centered approaches. The governance model remains nascent with limited institutional infrastructure for systematic AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: The African Union, G7, and UNDP are primary actors shaping Mauritius's AI policy landscape through regional coordination and capacity-building initiatives. Mauritius participates in these multilateral frameworks but lacks independent domestic AI governance institutions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with reliance on voluntary compliance and soft governance through international guidelines rather than domestic legal penalties or active regulatory oversight.

Regulation Status: Mexico has a draft AI regulation bill in progress based on a risk-based approach, aligned with EU and regional models. The Law for the Ethical Regulation of Artificial Intelligence remains in draft form as of March 2026, with no comprehensive binding legislation enacted.
Policy Lever: Mexico is establishing governance through a National Artificial Intelligence Commission and designating the Federal Telecommunications Institute (FTI) as the enforcement authority. Policy mechanisms rely on data protection frameworks and risk-based classification systems.
Governance Type: Mexico employs model governance through data protection and risk-based classification of AI applications. Governance structure is still under development with multiple institutional actors being coordinated through the nascent National AI Commission.
Actor Involvement: Mexico participates actively in regional governance through the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean, having signed the Santiago Declaration (2023) and the Ibero-American Charter of Artificial Intelligence in Public Administration (2023). Domestic actors include federal agencies, telecommunications regulators, and data protection authorities.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain underdeveloped as the primary legislation is still in draft form; soft enforcement through proposed institutional frameworks (FTI, National AI Commission) is anticipated but not yet operational.
Specific Laws: Law for the Ethical Regulation of Artificial Intelligence for the Mexican United States (2023, draft)

Regulation Status: Micronesia has no dedicated AI regulation or national AI policy as of March 2026. The country has not enacted specific legislation governing artificial intelligence systems or their deployment.
Policy Lever: No formal policy mechanisms or regulatory frameworks exist for AI governance. Micronesia relies on general data protection and ICT policies if applicable.
Governance Type: Governance structure for AI regulation is absent. Micronesia has not established dedicated agencies or inter-agency coordination bodies for AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Limited involvement from government, private sector, or international organizations in AI policy development within Micronesia. Participation primarily through regional Pacific island forums and international bodies.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI governance as regulatory frameworks are absent.

Regulation Status: Moldova applies the EU AI Act as part of its alignment with EU regulations through its association and eventual accession framework. The country implements a risk-based approach categorizing AI systems as prohibited, high-risk, or other systems with varying transparency obligations. National implementation measures and regulatory guidelines have been developed to operationalize EU AI Act provisions within Moldova's legal system.
Policy Lever: Moldova employs data protection, human rights safeguards, and institutional strengthening mechanisms aligned with EU frameworks to govern AI development and deployment.
