Governance Type: Governance structures for AI remain nascent, with no dedicated AI regulatory bodies or formal institutional frameworks established. Decision-making authority is dispersed across general ICT and communications ministries without specialized AI oversight.
Actor Involvement: Engagement is primarily at the national level with minimal involvement from private sector, academia, or international bodies. Tajikistan has limited resources and capacity for comprehensive AI governance coordination.
Enforcement Level: No active enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation, as formal regulatory frameworks have not been implemented.

Regulation Status: Tanzania remains in early-stage AI regulation development as of March 2026, with ongoing policy discussions through workshops and regional coordination but no comprehensive national AI legislation enacted. The country continues to work on risk-based frameworks addressing AI-generated content and related concerns through collaborative efforts.
Policy Lever: Primary policy levers focus on data governance and computing infrastructure management. Mechanisms remain largely advisory and voluntary, embedded within broader digital transformation and regional African Union initiatives.
Governance Type: Governance structure is predominantly organizational and multi-stakeholder, involving coordination between national authorities, regional bodies (African Union), and international partners. No dedicated standalone AI regulatory agency has been formally established.
Actor Involvement: Multiple actors are engaged including Tanzanian national authorities, the African Union, G7 observers, and UNDP technical support. International participation reflects broader continental and global AI governance alignment efforts.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms have been implemented; current efforts remain consultative and developmental with no penalty structures or active compliance monitoring systems in place.

Regulation Status: Thailand is developing a risk-based AI regulatory framework aligned with international standards, with a Draft Royal Decree on AI and AI regulation currently in progress. The approach mirrors the EU's risk-based model but remains in the draft stage without comprehensive enacted legislation.
Policy Lever: Thailand prioritizes AI as a strategic tool for research and development, economic innovation, and sustainable development objectives. Policy mechanisms include national AI strategies and support for R&D initiatives integrated with SDG alignment.
Governance Type: Thailand employs a case-by-case governance approach supported by multiple organizational bodies with emphasis on sustainable development principles. Governance is distributed across national agencies with coordination mechanisms for different AI application sectors.
Actor Involvement: Thailand actively engages with ASEAN regional coordination on AI governance while developing national regulations through government bodies. Multiple stakeholders including ministries, research institutions, and private sector actors participate in regulatory development.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain nascent as primary regulations are still in draft form, with limited formal enforcement infrastructure currently operational beyond advisory and consultation processes.
Specific Laws: Draft Royal Decree on AI (in progress), Draft AI Regulation (in progress)
Sources: Thailand Board of Investment|Thailand Ministry of Digital Economy and Society|ASEAN Secretariat AI governance initiatives

Regulation Status: Togo has minimal dedicated AI regulation as of March 2026, with no comprehensive national AI framework yet enacted. The country participates in African Union and regional AI governance initiatives but lacks independent binding AI legislation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data governance and digital infrastructure development through regional frameworks. Togo relies primarily on general ICT and data protection laws rather than AI-specific regulatory instruments.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily informal and organizationally fragmented, operating through government agencies and regional coordination bodies. Togo lacks a dedicated central AI authority or coordinating body with clear mandate.
Actor Involvement: Togo engages with the African Union, UNDP, and international development partners on AI policy, but primary involvement remains at continental and multilateral levels rather than national implementation. Limited direct G7 engagement specific to Togo.
Enforcement Level: No formal AI-specific enforcement mechanisms exist; enforcement relies on general ICT and data protection authorities with limited capacity and focus on AI issues.

Regulation Status: Tonga has no dedicated AI regulation framework as of March 2026. The country lacks specific legislation or formal policies addressing artificial intelligence technologies, relying instead on general ICT and data protection frameworks if applicable.
Policy Lever: No specific policy mechanisms for AI governance have been identified. Tonga's regulatory approach, if any, would fall under general telecommunications and digital governance structures rather than AI-specific levers.
Governance Type: Tonga has not established a formal governance structure specifically for AI regulation. Any oversight would theoretically occur through existing government ministries overseeing telecommunications and ICT sectors.
Actor Involvement: No identified national AI policy actors, coordination bodies, or formal stakeholder engagement mechanisms specific to AI governance in Tonga. Limited capacity and resources characteristic of small island developing states constrain domestic AI policy development.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms specific to AI regulation exist in Tonga.

Regulation Status: Trinidad and Tobago has not enacted dedicated AI-specific legislation as of 2026. The country relies primarily on existing data protection frameworks and participation in regional AI governance initiatives through IACLA, without comprehensive domestic AI regulation.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data protection and regional cooperation through the Santiago Declaration. Soft governance approaches and voluntary guidelines dominate, with limited sectoral regulation.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily organizational through regional association participation (IACLA) rather than through dedicated national AI governance bodies. Responsibility is dispersed across existing sectoral regulators without a centralized AI governance structure.
Actor Involvement: Trinidad and Tobago participates in the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and has signed the Santiago Declaration (2023). Regional coordination provides the primary governance framework, with limited national-level institutional actors.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, with no dedicated AI enforcement bodies or specific penalties for AI-related violations beyond general data protection provisions.

Regulation Status: Tunisia follows the African Union's AI regulatory framework initiatives without independent comprehensive national AI legislation as of March 2026. The country has adopted voluntary guidelines and participates in pan-African AI governance discussions but lacks binding domestic AI-specific laws.
Policy Lever: Tunisia relies primarily on African Union coordination mechanisms and sectoral regulations (data protection, telecom) rather than AI-specific policy instruments. Limited national AI strategy with emphasis on capacity building and alignment with African Union protocols.
Governance Type: Governance is decentralized across existing sectoral regulators (ANSI for digital security, telecom authority) without a dedicated AI oversight body. Tunisia coordinates through African Union structures and continental AI initiatives.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the African Union, Tunisian Ministry of Digital Affairs, and sectoral regulators, with limited private sector formal involvement in policymaking. Participation is primarily at the continental and ministerial levels.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement of AI governance is minimal, relying on existing sector regulators with unclear AI-specific mandates and limited dedicated resources.

Regulation Status: Turkey introduced its Artificial Intelligence Law in June 2024 and continues developing a comprehensive regulatory framework based on its National AI Strategy. The country is pursuing an innovative approach that balances AI development with risk-based safeguards, though full implementation remains in progress.
Policy Lever: Turkey employs risk-assessment requirements for AI developers and classifies systems as high-risk to mandate additional precautions. Data-centered requirements and privacy law reforms have been proposed to align with AI governance needs.
Governance Type: Governance is structured around core principles of security, transparency, fairness, accountability, and privacy with both national and organizational oversight mechanisms. Turkey is establishing institutional capacity for AI governance alongside international norm-setting.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include national government bodies and relevant ministries, with increasing regional and international engagement through participation in AI norms development. Turkey is positioned between domestic regulation and broader international AI governance frameworks.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are still being established with the recent legislation; specific enforcement capacity and penalty structures remain limited as regulations are transitioning from draft to implementation phase.
Specific Laws: Artificial Intelligence Law (2024)

Regulation Status: Turkmenistan has minimal formal AI regulation as of 2026, with a draft risk-based model law in development aimed at creating uniform rules for AI use in public relations. The regulatory framework remains nascent and largely focused on establishing foundational governance structures rather than comprehensive binding rules.
Policy Lever: AI is primarily positioned as a tool for research and development to support economic growth and regional innovation. Policy mechanisms emphasize technological development incentives rather than restrictive controls.
Governance Type: Governance follows a centralized national governmental model with case-by-case organizational rules and technological development zones designated for innovative economic growth. The structure prioritizes innovation space creation alongside emerging rule-making frameworks.
Actor Involvement: Involvement centers on national governmental actors with limited documented participation from private sector, international bodies, or civil society. Governance remains state-centric with regional economic development objectives.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal or absent, with no documented active oversight bodies, penalties, or compliance audits for AI-related activities.

Regulation Status: Tuvalu has not established dedicated AI regulation or a national AI policy framework as of March 2026. The country lacks specific legislation addressing artificial intelligence governance.
Policy Lever: No identified policy mechanisms, guidelines, or regulatory levers specific to AI have been implemented. Tuvalu relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks if applicable.
Governance Type: No dedicated AI governance structure exists. Any technology oversight would fall under general government ministries responsible for telecommunications or commerce.
Actor Involvement: Limited domestic capacity for AI regulation given Tuvalu's small size and population. International engagement through regional Pacific organizations may provide indirect influence on AI policy.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms specific to AI regulation are in place.

Regulation Status: Uganda has released non-binding best practice guidelines (GSR-24) through the ITU summit in 2024 and has shown political commitment to AI regulation through parliamentary calls for legislation. However, no comprehensive binding AI law has been enacted as of March 2026, with the country remaining in the legislative development phase.
Policy Lever: Uganda employs a data-driven policy approach with focus on AGI considerations, utilizing voluntary guidelines and calls for future legislation. The strategy emphasizes coordination between national bodies and international frameworks rather than mandatory enforcement mechanisms.
Governance Type: Uganda's governance structure involves coordination between case-by-case and organizational approaches spanning business and legal domains. Authority is distributed across national institutions with engagement from regional bodies including the African Union.
Actor Involvement: Uganda coordinates AI policy through multi-level governance involving national government (Parliamentary members like Tayebwa), regional actors (African Union), and international partners (G7, UNDP, ITU). This represents a hybrid national-regional-international approach characteristic of African digital policy development.
Enforcement Level: Current enforcement mechanisms remain minimal, with guidelines being advisory rather than legally binding and no formal enforcement infrastructure established for AI regulation as of March 2026.
Specific Laws: GSR-24 Best Practice Guidelines (2024)

Regulation Status: Ukraine has not yet enacted AI-specific legislation but has developed a regulatory roadmap through the Ministry of Digital Transformation. Guidelines for AI use in media have been published, and the government is actively working toward a comprehensive regulatory framework aligned with potential EU integration.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms include guidelines for media AI applications and emphasis on data-driven AI in defense/warfare contexts. The government is using roadmap-based approaches and white papers to shape future regulation rather than binding legislative instruments.
Governance Type: Governance is primarily centralized under the Ministry of Digital Transformation with case-by-case organizational oversight. Authority structures remain nascent as formal regulatory institutions for AI are still being established.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors are governmental bodies at the national level, particularly the Ministry of Digital Transformation. Future governance may shift if Ukraine integrates with EU regulatory frameworks, introducing supranational oversight.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms are minimal, limited to voluntary compliance with guidelines rather than binding legal penalties or active monitoring.

Regulation Status: The UAE maintains a forward-looking, risk-based approach to AI governance without comprehensive sector-wide legislation. The government has released multiple AI ethics guidelines, sectoral frameworks for financial services and public sector AI use, and continues developing regulatory mechanisms through its AI Council and strategic initiatives.
Policy Lever: The UAE employs data-centered and guidelines-based policy mechanisms, including financial institution oversight for AI safety and establishing research infrastructure. Policy levers focus on voluntary compliance frameworks, ethical guidelines, and sectoral-specific regulations rather than binding statutory requirements.
Governance Type: Governance is structured through the UAE AI Council, a dedicated Minister of State for Artificial Intelligence (established 2017), and sector-specific organizational oversight bodies. The government has enacted directives on safe AI use and maintains coordinated governance across federal and emirate-level entities.
Actor Involvement: The UAE Government is recognized as one of the world's most proactive governments on AI policy with international engagement. Actors include government ministries, financial regulatory bodies, public sector organizations, and participation in global AI governance discussions.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains relatively soft, relying primarily on compliance monitoring within specific sectors like financial services and public administration rather than penalties and systematic audits.
Specific Laws: UAE Artificial Intelligence Law (governance framework on safe AI use)|UAE AI Ethics Framework

Regulation Status: The UK has not yet enacted AI-specific legislation as of March 2026, though the AI (Regulation) Bill was reintroduced following the 2024 general election. The government continues to pursue a sector-specific regulatory approach guided by AI ethics principles rather than comprehensive standalone AI legislation.
Policy Lever: The UK employs a multi-faceted approach combining sectoral regulation through existing frameworks, data protection (GDPR/UK-GDPR), and principles-based guidance. Policy mechanisms include attracting FDI and R&D through competitive positioning and establishing governance through the UK AI Safety Institute.
Governance Type: The UK operates through a decentralized, sector-specific regulatory model applying AI ethics principles across different industries rather than a single unified AI regulator. The UK AI Safety Institute serves as a research and advisory body to inform sectoral regulators.
Actor Involvement: The UK maintains significant international leadership through hosting the Global AI Safety Summit (2023), co-authoring the Bletchley Declaration, and operating the UK AI Safety Institute. Multiple domestic actors including sectoral regulators (ICO, FCA, CMA) and government departments are engaged in AI governance.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains limited to existing sectoral regulators applying current frameworks (data protection, competition law) with no dedicated AI enforcement mechanism or dedicated AI regulator with binding powers.
Specific Laws: Data Protection Act 2018, Online Safety Bill 2023

Regulation Status: The US maintains a fragmented, risk-based approach to AI regulation with Executive Order 14110 (2023) providing federal guidance, while states like California, Virginia, and Maryland lead with direct AI legislation. The regulatory landscape continues evolving from voluntary frameworks toward more prescriptive requirements, particularly for high-risk applications like deepfakes and financial services.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data protection, computing standards, and sector-specific regulation (deepfakes, robocalls, financial services, housing). The FCC, House Financial Services Committee, and state-level bodies employ transparency requirements, content verification mandates, and disclosure rules as primary levers.
Governance Type: Governance operates through a mixed federal-state model with multiple agencies (FCC, CFPB, SEC, FTC) and state legislatures acting independently, coordinated through executive orders and informal inter-agency frameworks. There is emerging focus on transparency in elections and democratic integrity as governance priorities.
Actor Involvement: Multiple federal agencies, state governments, and private sector actors drive regulation; the US emphasizes maintaining technological leadership while engaging G7 and EU partners on AI standards and competition frameworks. International coordination through joint statements on generative AI foundation models reflects both competitive and collaborative postures.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains inconsistent across sectors, with proposed rules (robocalls, financial services) not yet fully implemented; existing enforcement relies on FTC actions and state-level penalties, but systematic auditing and penalties for non-compliance are still developing.
Specific Laws: Executive Order 14110 (2023), Executive Order 13859 (2019), Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights (2022), Transparency in Elections Act (proposed 2024)

Regulation Status: Uruguay has released AI regulatory recommendations through Agesic (July 2024) and is developing a national AI strategy with emphasis on public sector governance. The country is in the draft legislation phase with no comprehensive binding law yet enacted, though implementation frameworks are being developed.
Policy Lever: Uruguay is establishing public databases and training datasets for AI systems, with focus on data governance and algorithm transparency. Internal data committees are being created within public entities to manage AI-related data issues.
Governance Type: Uruguay is strengthening Agesic's competencies on AI and data regulation with emphasis on public sector leadership. The governance model prioritizes creating internal data committees within government entities rather than a single independent regulatory agency.
Actor Involvement: Uruguay participates in regional frameworks including the Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and has signed the 2023 Santiago Declaration on Ethical AI. National incentives align with regional commitments while maintaining domestic policy priorities.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain limited as Uruguay is still in the recommendations and strategy development phase, with no formal enforcement bodies or penalties established yet.

Regulation Status: Uzbekistan maintains a risk-based approach to AI regulation centered on experimental innovation regimes rather than comprehensive binding legislation. The 2021 resolution established a special framework for AI technologies within R&D contexts, with ongoing development of broader regulatory architecture aligned with international standards.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on AI as an R&D tool through innovation zones and experimental regimes rather than prescriptive sectoral regulation. Data governance and AI development are promoted via targeted incentives for research institutions and technology parks.
Governance Type: Governance remains primarily case-by-case and organizationally fragmented, with national governmental actors leading through the Ministry of Innovative Development and various sectoral agencies. Coordination between agencies appears limited with no unified AI regulatory authority established.
Actor Involvement: Central governmental actors, particularly at the national level, drive AI policy through innovation initiatives and R&D frameworks. Private sector and international partners have minimal formal involvement in regulatory decision-making processes.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain underdeveloped with limited monitoring, audit capacity, or penalties for non-compliance outside experimental innovation zones.
Specific Laws: Resolution on measures to introduce special regime for use of AI technologies (2021)

Regulation Status: Vanuatu has no dedicated AI regulation or national AI policy framework as of March 2026. The country has not enacted specific legislation governing artificial intelligence development or deployment.
Policy Lever: No formal policy mechanisms or regulatory levers for AI governance are currently in place. Vanuatu relies on general ICT and data protection frameworks that predate modern AI concerns.
Governance Type: There is no established governance structure specifically for AI regulation. Oversight would fall under general government ministries responsible for communications or commerce if any AI-related issues arise.
Actor Involvement: No documented involvement of government agencies, private sector bodies, or civil society organizations in coordinated AI governance efforts. International participation in AI policy discussions remains minimal.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI-specific regulations given the absence of dedicated legislation.

Regulation Status: Vatican City has not enacted specific AI regulation as of March 2026. The Holy See has engaged in ethical discourse on AI through papal statements and Vatican documents, but lacks formal binding legislation governing AI development or deployment within its territory.
Policy Lever: Vatican City relies on ethical guidance and soft power through papal encyclicals and Vatican dicastery positions rather than statutory regulation. International advocacy and participation in UN and UNESCO discussions on AI ethics serve as primary policy mechanisms.
Governance Type: Governance is handled through ecclesiastical structures, primarily via the Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development and papal pronouncements. No dedicated AI regulatory body or national AI strategy exists.
Actor Involvement: Primary actors include the Pope, Vatican dicasteries, and participation in international forums such as the UN and UNESCO. The scope is largely limited to theological and ethical advocacy rather than territorial governance enforcement.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation within Vatican City, relying instead on moral authority and international influence.

Regulation Status: Venezuela has not enacted dedicated AI-specific legislation or national AI policies as of March 2026. The country lacks formal regulatory frameworks governing artificial intelligence development, deployment, or use.
Policy Lever: No identified policy mechanisms or regulatory levers specific to AI have been implemented. Venezuela's participation in regional initiatives remains limited to voluntary, non-binding declarations.
Governance Type: Venezuela's AI governance exists primarily through regional association participation rather than domestic institutional structures. No dedicated national AI governance bodies or oversight agencies have been established.
Actor Involvement: Venezuela's primary AI policy involvement is through the regional Intergovernmental Council on Artificial Intelligence for Latin America and the Caribbean and the 2023 Santiago Declaration on ethical AI. Limited domestic stakeholder engagement in AI policy development is evident.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation in Venezuela due to the absence of binding domestic legislation or regulatory frameworks.

Regulation Status: Vietnam has a draft law on digital technology covering AI under public consultation as of early 2026, with concurrent draft standards on AI also undergoing public review. The country is in the active drafting phase rather than having enacted comprehensive binding legislation, reflecting a transitional regulatory approach.
Policy Lever: Vietnam employs data and algorithm export controls alongside federal spending initiatives to support sector growth, combining restrictive and developmental policy mechanisms. These levers are part of a broader digital technology strategy rather than AI-specific regulatory instruments.
Governance Type: Vietnam's AI governance involves case-by-case oversight of social media platforms, coordination with policymakers, and engagement with tech companies, supplemented by organizational frameworks. The approach is multi-stakeholder but not yet formalized through dedicated AI regulatory bodies.
Actor Involvement: Key actors include the Vietnamese government, tech companies, and regional ASEAN coordination mechanisms, positioning AI regulation within broader Southeast Asian digital governance initiatives. Regional cooperation through ASEAN provides an additional governance layer beyond national boundaries.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement mechanisms remain limited due to the draft status of AI legislation, with soft oversight through case-based reviews and industry engagement rather than formal penalties or audits.
Specific Laws: Draft Law on Digital Technology (2026, under consultation)

Regulation Status: Yemen has no specific AI legislation or regulatory framework as of March 2026. Ongoing political fragmentation and conflict continue to severely limit governmental capacity to develop or implement technology policy.
Policy Lever: No policy mechanisms, guidelines, or regulatory levers for AI governance are currently in place. Government institutions lack resources for technology regulation.
Governance Type: No formal AI governance structure exists in Yemen. The fragmented political situation with competing authorities prevents establishment of centralized technology oversight.
Actor Involvement: No documented AI governance actors or institutional involvement at national level. International actors provide limited technical assistance given security constraints.
Enforcement Level: No enforcement mechanisms exist for AI regulation due to state capacity limitations and ongoing conflict.

Regulation Status: Zambia is in the early-to-intermediate stages of AI regulation development, with a national AI strategy framework emerging alongside broader ICT sector governance. The country is leveraging existing cybersecurity legislation while developing sector-specific AI policies through international partnerships.
Policy Lever: Zambia employs data protection, computing infrastructure standards, and high-risk AI use case frameworks as primary policy mechanisms. The approach integrates transparency requirements and bias mitigation measures within organizational governance structures.
Governance Type: Governance operates through case-by-case and organizational-level oversight mechanisms emphasizing transparency and public accountability. Institutional responsibility is distributed across national bodies with international stakeholder coordination.
Actor Involvement: Multi-layered governance involving national authorities, African Union bodies, UNDP, G7 coordination, and EU partnership for startup innovation frameworks. International collaboration extends to bilateral agreements focused on technological capacity building and regulatory harmonization.
Enforcement Level: Enforcement remains limited, relying primarily on existing cybersecurity frameworks with emerging oversight mechanisms for AI-specific applications still under development.
Specific Laws: Cyber Security and Cyber Crimes Act

Regulation Status: Zimbabwe has called for AI-specific regulation aligned with its Institutional Strategic Plan but has not yet enacted comprehensive AI legislation as of March 2026. The country maintains a risk-based approach framework that was articulated in early 2024, with ongoing policy development rather than binding regulatory instruments.
Policy Lever: Policy mechanisms focus on data governance and computing infrastructure, with stated intentions to establish tools for high-risk AI use cases. However, implementation of these mechanisms remains limited without formal legislative backing.
Governance Type: Governance structure emphasizes case-by-case and organizational approaches with transparency and bias mitigation as stated principles. However, formal institutional oversight bodies for AI regulation have not been clearly established or are still in development.
Actor Involvement: Zimbabwe engages with national regulatory efforts alongside regional and international actors including the African Union, G7 frameworks, and UNDP. These partnerships inform policy direction but decision-making authority remains primarily national.
Enforcement Level: No formal enforcement mechanisms for AI regulation have been implemented, given the absence of binding legislation.
